7.1.5 Setting Encrypted Password for GRUB2
Description
Grand unified bootloader (GRUB) is a boot manager for operating systems (OSs)
such as Windows and Linux. GRUB2 is a later version of GRUB.
When starting the system, you can modify the startup parameters of the system
on the GRUB2 interface. To prevent unauthorized modification of the startup
parameters of the system, you need to encrypt the GRUB2 interface. In this way,
the startup parameters can be modified only when you enter the correct GRUB2
password.
NO TE
The default password of grub2 is Changeme_123. You are advised to change the default
password upon the first login and periodically update the password. If the password is
leaked, startup item configurations may be modified, causing the system startup failure.
Implementation
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
For details, see 13.1 Logging In to a Server Using PuTTY.
Step 2 Run the following command to generate an encrypted password:
$ su - root
Password:password for root

Confirm password:
NO TE
SHA-512 is used as the GRUB2 encryption algorithm.
Step 3 Run the cat command to view the grub.cfg file.
# cd /boot/efi/EFI/euleros/
# cat grub.cfg
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub2-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
set pager=1
...
terminal_output console
if [ x$feature_timeout_style = xy ] ; then
set timeout_style=menu
set timeout=5
# Fallback normal timeout code in case the timeout_style feature is
# unavailable.
else
set timeout=5
fi
set superusers=root
password_pbkdf2 root
grub.pbkdf2.sha512.10000.D4D775602C4E9F76EF4A9A6E726486941C8AAFB4762227E6973690ED5A760D59
247E7E6ECA72472FBEEBFD9DB60F8EE56A4078094542C790BF0967879BE2D60C.B2742F38995B4B716EA7B0
E639D02BE6C4E649E30576E5F5505B85844172B831841DA80D264FD14B025F3C8804158E7FC082998664BD
03A92663FB4CE293807B
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/01_menu_auto_hide ###
if [ "${boot_success}" = "1" -o "${boot_indeterminate}" = "1" ]; then
set last_boot_ok=1
else
set last_boot_ok=0
fi
# Reset boot_indeterminate after a successful boot
if [ "${boot_success}" = "1" ] ; then
set boot_indeterminate=0
# Avoid boot_indeterminate causing the menu to be hidden more then once
elif [ "${boot_indeterminate}" = "1" ]; then
set boot_indeterminate=2
fi
set boot_success=0
save_env boot_success boot_indeterminate
if [ x$feature_timeout_style = xy ] ; then
if [ "${menu_show_once}" ]; then
unset menu_show_once
save_env menu_show_once
set timeout_style=menu
set timeout=60
elif [ "${menu_auto_hide}" -a "${last_boot_ok}" = "1" ]; then
set orig_timeout_style=${timeout_style}
set orig_timeout=${timeout}
if [ "${fastboot}" = "1" ]; then
# timeout_style=menu + timeout=0 avoids the countdown code keypress check
set timeout_style=menu

set timeout_style=hidden
set timeout=1
fi
fi
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/01_menu_auto_hide ###
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/01_users ###
if [ -f ${prefix}/user.cfg ]; then
source ${prefix}/user.cfg
if [ -n "${GRUB2_PASSWORD}" ]; then
set superusers="root"
export superusers
password_pbkdf2 root ${GRUB2_PASSWORD}
fi
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/01_users ###
...
NO TE
● The superusers field is used to set the account name of the super GRUB2 administrator.
● Following the password_pbkdf2 field, the first parameter is the GRUB2 account name,
and the second parameter is the ciphertext password of the account.
● Currently, GRUB2 menu management commands including grub2-mkconfig cannot be
used in the AARCH64 architecture.
----End
7.1.6 Configuring the Hacker Language Dictionary
A hacker language dictionary is used to store character conversion rules.
Passwords are converted based on the rules. If a password converted using the
hacker language dictionary is in the password dictionary, do not set the password
as the user password to ensure user account security. This section describes how
to update the hacker language dictionary that does not meet user requirements.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossuser users for logging in
to the node where SMLogLic resides.
Context
● The hacker language dictionary defines the rules of converting a character or
string into an uppercase or lowercase letter. For example, if a rule for
converting the string |-|1234 a into x exists in the hacker language dictionary
and x exists in the password dictionary file, a password cannot be set to |-|
1234 abcd! when Password cannot contain words in password dictionary
and Password complies with requirements in hacker language dictionary
are enabled.
● A hacker dictionary file can contain multiple rules. In each rule, the string on
the left of the last equal sign (=) is replaced with the first letter on the right
of the last equal sign. For example, |-|1234 a = x indicates that |-|1234 a in
the password will be replaced with x.
● In a hacker dictionary file, each row contains only one rule. Hacker dictionary

strings on the left and right sides of the last equal sign are filtered out.
For example, x=x = y indicates that x=x will be replaced with y.
– After the filtering, the first character of the string retaining on the right
of the last equal sign must be a letter and the string retaining on the left
of the last equal sign will be replaced with this letter. For example, x=x =
ya indicates that x=x will be replaced with y.
Procedure
Step 1 On the local PC, create a hacker language dictionary file in .txt format. Ensure
that the file size does not exceed 200 KB. Configure a conversion rule in the file.
The following is an example:
1234 a = x
x=%x = 2ya
user = y
Step 2 Use FileZilla to log in to the node where SMLogLic resides, as the sopuser user in
SFTP mode. Upload the hacker language dictionary file to the /home/sopuser
directory. For details, see 13.2 Transferring Files Using FileZilla.
Step 3 Use PuTTY to log in to the node where SMLogLic resides, as the sopuser user in
SSH mode. For details about how to obtain the IP address of the node where a
service resides, see 12.3 Querying the Management IP Address of the Node
Where a Process or Service Resides.
Step 4 Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
$ su - ossuser
Password: password for the ossuser user
Step 5 Run the following commands to change the owner and permission of the hacker
language dictionary file:
$ cp /home/sopuser/<hacker language dictionary file> /home/ossuser/<hacker
language dictionary file>
$ chown ossuser:ossgroup <hacker language dictionary file>
$ chmod 600 <hacker language dictionary file>
Step 6 Run the following command to go to the target directory:
$ cd /opt/oss/NetEco/apps/SMLogLicService/bin
Step 7 Run the following command to update the hacker language dictionary:
$ ./updateComplexDic.sh -file /home/ossuser/hacker language dictionary file
● If the following information is displayed, the hacker language dictionary is
successfully updated:
Successfully updated 1 lines of complex dictionary data.
● Otherwise, the hacker language dictionary fails to be updated. Check whether
the hacker language dictionary meets requirements. If it does not, modify the
language dictionary and try again. If yes, contact technical support to
troubleshoot the failure.

Step 9 Run the following command to delete the file in the /home/sopuser directory:
$ cd /home/sopuser
$ rm -rf <hacker language dictionary file>
----End
7.2 Web User Management of the PowerEcho
Security management prevents unauthorized users from accessing the system and
ensures system data security.
7.2.1 Overview
Web user security management includes permission management, user
monitoring and maintenance, password and account policies, access control
management, and remote authentication.
Panorama
Figure 7-15 shows the user security management operations.
Figure 7-15 User security management panorama
Basic Concepts
Table 7-14 describes the basic concepts of user security management.

Concept Description
Permission A permission defines what operations a user can
perform on what objects.
Permission elements include an operator, operation
objects, and operations.
● Permissions are granted to users.
● Operation objects include the system (the
PowerEcho) and managed objects (physical and
virtual managed objects, such as servers, network
devices, and VMs) where users perform operations.
● Operations include operations on the system and
managed objects.
User A user refers to the person who uses the system.
Information about a user includes a username,
password, and permissions.
Users can be classified into the following types:
● Local users: users who are authenticated and log in
to the PowerEcho
● Third-party users: users (machine-machine
accounts) for interconnecting the PowerEcho with
third-party systems
● Remote users: users for interconnecting with
Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) or
Remote Authentication Dial In User Service
(RADIUS) servers

Concept Description
Role A role is a collection of permissions and managed
objects.
The system provides default roles. In addition, the
permissions of the default roles are predefined by the
system and cannot be modified.
Figure 7-16 shows the relationship among the
permissions of the roles.
● Administrators: Users attached to this role have
permission to perform operations except managing
users, querying security logs, querying personal
security logs, and viewing online users.
● SMManagers: Users attached to this role have
permission to manage users, query security logs,
view online users, and update ACL policies.
● Operators: Users attached to this role have
permission to perform operations of backup and
restore, deployment, system monitoring,
maintenance, system settings, and alarms.
● Monitors: Users attached to this role have
permission to monitor operations of backup and
restore, deployment, system monitoring,
maintenance, system settings, and alarms.
Operation set An operation set is a collection of operation rights. A
security administrator can assign an operation set to a
role to grant all users attached to the role all operation
rights of the operation set.
Figure 7-16 Permission relationship among default user roles
● System administrator: The system administrator has all permissions on all
managed objects, and is attached to both the Administrators and

NO TICE
The system administrator has the highest permission for the system and all
managed objects. Exercise caution when performing operations as this user,
and do not perform any operations that hinder the system security. For
example, do not share or distribute the username and password of the system
administrator.
● Administrator: A user attached to the Administrators role is an administrator
and has all permissions of the Administrators role.
● Security administrator: A user attached to the SMManagers role is a security
administrator and has all permissions of the SMManagers role.
● Operator: A user attached to the Operators role is an operator and has all
permissions of the Operators role.
● Monitor: A user attached to the Monitors role is an operator and has all
permissions of the Monitors role.
Security policy
Table 7-15 describes the operations related to security policies of user security
management.
Table 7-15 Operations
Operation Description
Setting the account policy The account policy specifies the minimum
length of a username, account login, and
lockout policies. The account policy can
improve the security of user accounts.
Setting the password policy The password policy specifies the password
requirements, such as password complexity,
password change interval, and character
restrictions. The password policy prevents users
from using simple passwords or using the
same passwords for a long time.
Setting client IP address A client IP address control policy controls the
control policies range of IP addresses that a user can use to
log in to the PowerEcho.
Setting login time policies The login time policy controls the time range
during which a user can log in to the
PowerEcho.
Setting the verification code You can configure the verification code policy
policy to control the verification type displayed on
pages such as the login page. Appropriate
settings can improve the system security. The
verification code policy takes effect on all
users.

Multi-user mode and single-user mode are available for system login. In most
cases, the system runs in multi-user mode. If you need to perform maintenance
operations on the PowerEcho (for example, adjusting the role to which a user is
attached, managed objects, or operation rights), switch to the single-user mode to
prevent other users' operations from affecting the system maintenance operations.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > Authentication from the menu.
Step 2 On the System Login Mode page, select a login mode.
NO TE
● Switching to Single-user mode will log out all the sessions except your session.
Therefore, exercise caution when performing this operation. However, third-party users
can log in.
● If Single-user mode is selected, you can set Switching duration. The value 0 indicates
that the system immediately switches to the single-user mode. Other values indicate
that the system switches to the single-user mode after the specified period of time.
● You are advised to switch the system to the multi-user mode immediately after finishing
maintenance operations so that other users can use the system.
Step 3 Click Apply.
----End
7.2.3 Creating Users
After a security administrator creates a user and attaches the user to a role, the
user has the permissions of this role.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Precautions
For security purposes, the PowerEcho accounts are used only by maintenance
personnel of the system. Do not disclose the accounts to external organizations or
third-party personnel.
Disabling a user will force online sessions of the user on the PowerEcho to be

Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Users.
Step 3 On the Users page, perform the following operations as prompted based on the
number of users to be created:
● To create a single user, click Create.
NO TE
– You can quickly create a user by clicking Copy in the Operation column of a user
and adjusting the information as required. The system administrator user has
permission to perform security operations and therefore cannot be copied.
– When creating a user as the system administrator user, you are advised not to
attach the new user to both the Administrators and SMManagers roles to ensure
system security. Users attached to both the Administrators and SMManagers
roles have the maximum permission on all resources in the system. Therefore,
perform operations using these user accounts with caution. Do not perform any
operations affecting system security. For example, do not share or distribute these
usernames and passwords.
– If Two-factor authentication is enabled, enter Mobile number or Email address.
When logging in to the system, you need to obtain an SMS verification code or
email verification code for two-factor authentication.
● To create users in batches, click and choose Import Users.
----End
Related Tasks
For details about other operations, see 7.2.6.1 Common Operations for User
Information Maintenance.
7.2.4 Adjusting Permission After Changing Role of a User
If the position of an employee has changed, security administrators need to adjust
the role to which the account of the employee is attached to change the
permissions of the employee.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Context
A position change occurs in the following situations:
● An employee is recruited.
● The role of an employee (as a user) is changed.

Disabling or deleting a user will force online sessions of the user on the
PowerEcho to be logged out. Exercise caution when performing this operation.
Procedure
● An employee is recruited.
a. Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the
menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Users.
c. On the Users page, click Create.
d. On the displayed page, set basic user information and click Next.
e. Select the role to which the user is attached and click Next.
f. Set access control information about the user and click OK.
● The role of an employee (as a user) is changed.
a. Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the
menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Users.
c. Click the name of the user whose role is changed and go to the Roles tab
page.
d. Click Edit in the upper right corner of the page.
e. Add or delete the role of a user based on the user position changes.
● An employee resigns.
a. Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the
menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Users. Perform the following operations
based on whether the employee account needs to be retained.
▪
If you want to retain the account, click Disable in the Operation
column of the row that contains the user to disable the user.
▪
If you do not want to retain the account, click Delete in the
Operation column of the row that contains the user to delete the
user.
7.2.5 Monitoring Users
Online user sessions can be monitored so that security administrators can learn
about the online users in the system, and the access addresses, access time, and
roles of these users. When detecting an unauthorized user operation, security
administrators can send a message to the user or forcibly log out the user.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the

● A user session refers to a connection between a user and the system. A
session starts when the user logs in to the system and ends when the user
logs out of the system. The maximum number of online sessions for a user is
specified by the Max. online sessions parameter. Online sessions of local
users and remote users (excluding third-party users) consume license resource
control item.
● Users' personal information is not monitored during session monitoring.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Online Users.
The information about all online users is displayed in the list.
NO TE
Client Name indicates the name of the client where this session is generated.
Step 3 Click Monitor in the Operation column of the target user to view the status of
the user.
● The User Operations list displays only operations performed after the Online
Users page is opened. Operations of the monitored users are updated in the
User Operations list in real time.
● Level indicates the risk level of an operation, including Risk, Minor, and
Warning. The value of Level is the same as that recorded in operation logs.
Step 4 To send a message to a user, click Send Message in the Operation column of the
user. Alternatively, select multiple users and click Send Message above the user
list to send messages to these users.
Step 5 When detecting that a user is performing risky operations, click Log Out in the
Operation column of the user to forcibly log out the user. Alternatively, select
multiple users and click Log Out above the user list to force a logout of these
users in batches.
NO TICE
This operation involves user login status and may force a logout of logged-in
users. Therefore, exercise caution when performing this operation.
----End

7.2.6.2 Creating a Role and Granting Permissions
If the default roles provided by the system cannot meet user authorization
requirements in the authorization plan, security administrators need to create
roles and grant them permissions based on the plan.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Context
● If only a few roles are required, create them one by one.
● If multiple roles are required or the roles are obtained from the files exported
from other systems, create roles in batches.
Procedure
● Creating a single role
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Roles.
Step 3 On the Roles page, click Create.
NO TE
You can quickly create a role by clicking Copy in the Operation column of a role and
modifying the information as required.
Step 4 On the page that is displayed, set basic role information.
Step 5 Select the users to be attached to the role.
After role authorization is complete, the users you have selected have the

application-level permissions of the role based on the authorization plan for
application-level permissions of the role.
Step 7 Click OK.
----End
● Creating roles in batches
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Roles.
Step 3 On the Roles page, click and choose Batch Import Roles.
Step 4 In the Batch Import Roles dialog box, click the template name to download the
template, and fill in the template.
NO TE
● The system provides two template formats: Role Template.xls and Role Template.xlsx,
and you can edit the template in .csv format. Select a template format as required.
● If you need to import the associated permissions, you can refer to the exported .zip
package that contains associated permission information as the template (file format,
file name, and table template) of the file to be imported.
Step 5 Set Import associated permissions and Permission import mode based on the
type of the file to be imported.
● If you need to import associated permissions, ensure that the file to be
imported is in .zip format and contains the information about associated
permissions of the roles.
● If Permission import mode is set to Based on file data or Combined, the
existing data in the system will be modified in batches and cannot be
restored. You are advised to export the existing data for backup in advance.
Step 6 Click the folder icon. In the displayed dialog box, select the edited template.
Step 7 Click Create.
After roles are imported, you can perform the following operations:
1. On the displayed page, view the number of successfully imported roles and
the number of roles that fail to be imported.
2. In the Result list, view the imported roles and their details.
3. If partial failure occurs, modify the role information that failed to be imported
based on the details, and import them again.
4. Click Download Report to download the report for this import. The report
cannot be downloaded after you close the dialog box.
Step 8 Click OK.
Step 9 Grant permissions to the roles created in batches based on the authorization plan.

role.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
If a user logs in to a third-party system in SSO mode, role information (excluding
permissions) about this user can be synchronized to the third-party system. To
ensure that this user has the same permissions on the third-party system as those
on the system, create the same role for the user on the third-party system and
bind the same permissions to the role.
Related Tasks
For details about how to maintain role information, see 7.2.6.3 Common
Operations for Role Information Maintenance.

operation rights and clicking Delete.

7.2.6.6 Modifying User Information in Batches
Security administrators can modify user information (such as Max. online
sessions and Login Time Policy) in batches, improving system security.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.

You can use the following methods to modify user information in batches:
● Using the Batch Modify Users function
Select the users whose information needs to be modified and click Batch
Modify Users.
● Modifying an exported user information file
Export user information as a file, modify the file, and then import the
modified file to the system.
Precautions
The operations involve user permission adjustment, which may force online users
to be logged out. Therefore, exercise caution when performing this operation.
Procedure
● Using the Batch Modify Users function
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Users.
Step 3 In the user list, select one or more users, click , and choose Batch Modify
Users.
The information about the system administrator and the user you are currently
logged in as cannot be modified. The information about a maximum of 100 users
can be modified at a time.
Step 4 On the User List tab page, confirm the users whose information needs to be
modified. Then, click Next.
To modify a user in User List, perform the following operations:
● If some users to be modified are not in the list, click Cancel and select the
users to be modified again.
● If some users who do not need to be modified in the list are selected, click
Delete in the Operation column of the rows that contain these users to
delete them.
Step 5 Select the user information to be modified on the Modification Items page as
required. Click OK. On the Modification Result page, view the modification
results and details.
If the existing time policies do not meet the requirements, click Create to create a
policy and then click Refresh. Then, you can select the new time policy from the
time policy list.
NO TE
● After the login time policy is modified, the users who do not meet the policy
requirements will be forcibly logged out if Log out of sessions that do not comply
with client IP address or login time policies is selected in the account policy.
● When modifying information in batches, you can modify the two-factor authentication

----End
● Modifying an exported user information file
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Users.
Step 3 Select the users whose information needs to be exported, click , and choose
Export Selected Users or Export All Users.
Step 4 In the Select File Format dialog box, select a file format and click OK.
Step 5 Modify the exported user information file.
● When modifying the user information file, do not change the sequence of the
fields in the file.
● If you change the value of a field to empty or an invalid value, the value of
this field will not be changed.
● The following user information cannot be modified:
– Type and Last Login of all users
– Information about the user you are logged in as and the system
administrator user
– Mobile Number, Email Address, Password, Login Time Policy, Client IP
Address Policy, and Password Validity Period (Days) of remote users
– Roles, Login Time Policy, and Allowed Logins of default users
– Password and Auto-Logout If No Activity Within of third-party users
Step 6 Click and choose Import Users.
Step 7 In the Import Users window, choose Update Users to import users.
Step 8 Click the folder icon, select the modified user information file, and click Modify.
Step 9 After the import is complete:
● On the displayed page, view the number of successfully imported users and
the number of users who fail to be imported.
● In the Result list, view the imported users and their details.
Step 10 Click OK.
----End
7.2.6.7 Changing Personal Passwords
If passwords are disclosed or remain unchanged for a long time, users can change
their personal passwords by setting personal information. For user security
purposes, you need to change your passwords periodically.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the

● If you cannot change your password, contact security administrators.
● If Log out session after password change is enabled in the password policy,
all sessions of the user are logged out after the user changes the password on
the Personal Settings page. Exercise caution when performing this operation.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > Change Password from the menu.
Step 2 Perform operations as prompted.
NO TE
User information is more secure if a password is changed more frequently. If a user forgets
the password due to frequent password changes, contact security administrators to reset
the password.
----End
7.2.6.8 Resetting a User Password
If a user other than the system administrator user forgets the password or cannot
change the password, this user needs to contact security administrators to reset
the password. For account security purposes, it is recommended that third-party
users contact security administrators to periodically reset their passwords.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
To reset the password for a security administrator, you have logged in to the
PowerEcho as the system administrator user.
Precautions
If a user has logged in to the PowerEcho and Force logout after password
change in the password policy is selected, resetting the password for the user will
force the user to log out. Therefore, exercise caution when performing this
operation.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Users.
Step 3 Click Reset Password in the Operation column of the row that contains the user
and reset the password.

NO TICE
You are not allowed to reset the password for the system administrator user. The
password for the system administrator user cannot be retrieved. You can reset the
password for the system administrator user only by reinstalling the system.
Therefore, remember the password.
----End
7.2.6.9 Unlocking IP Addresses
After an IP address is locked, users cannot log in to the system from the IP
address. The IP address will be automatically unlocked after the period of time
displayed on the login page. Alternatively, users can contact a security
administrator to unlock the IP address immediately so that they can log in to the
system from the IP address.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Context
Security administrators can use IP address lockout management to view and
unlock locked IP addresses. IP address lockout is classified into the following types,
and a maximum of 500 records can be displayed for each type:
● IP address lockout: All users cannot log in from locked IP addresses.
● Default user and IP address lockout: If a default user and an IP address are
locked, the default user cannot log in from the IP address. Other users can log
in from the IP address, and the default user can log in from other IP
addresses.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose IP Address Lockout.
Step 3 On the IP Address Lockout page, unlock IP addresses as required.
● Search for a desired IP address in the search box, and click Unlock in the
Operation column of the row that contains the IP address.
● Select multiple IP addresses and click Unlock to the upper right of the list.
----End
7.2.7 User Policy Management (the PowerEcho)
User policies allow you to set access control rules for users. This function improves
O&M efficiency and prevents unauthorized users from accessing the system to
ensure system security.

Security administrators set login or lockout policies for user accounts as needed to
improve system access security. The account policy takes effect for all users.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Policies from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Account Policy.
Step 3 On the Account Policy page, set the account policy.
NO TE
● If Enable the user policy if no login within a period is selected, the system will
automatically delete the users who meet the policy. Perform this operation with caution.
● Auto-logout if no activity within: If a user does not perform any operation within the
period specified by this parameter, the user will be logged out. The setting takes effect
only for local and remote users and does not take effect for third-party users. If this
parameter is set to Unlimited, user sessions will not be automatically logged out.
● If Show warning upon successful login is selected, a login warning message is
displayed to notify users of rules that should be obeyed after users log in to the system.
The warning message provides legal declaration. Security administrators can customize
this warning message based on user management regulation.
● The Allow users to modify personal two-factor authentication settings item in the
account policy controls whether users can modify the two-factor authentication setting
on the Modify Personal Info page.
● To improve account security, you are advised to enable all the items in the account
policy.
Step 4 Click Apply.
----End
7.2.7.2 Setting the Password Policy
Security administrators set the password complexity, change interval, and
character limitation based on site requirements to prevent users from setting weak
passwords or using a password for a long period of time, improving system access
security. The password policy takes effect for all users.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Precautions
● If you disable the password complexity policy items and the item of forcibly
changing passwords after they expire, user security weakens. You are advised

prevent accounts from being stolen. Exercise caution when modifying a
password change interval and disabling the function of periodically changing
passwords.
● A new password policy does not affect the existing passwords.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Policies from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Password Policy.
Step 3 On the Password Policy page, set the password policy. Some of the policy
parameters are described in Table 7-19.
Table 7-19 Parameter description
Parameter Description
Min. password usage After Min. password usage period (days) or Enforce
period (days) password expiration is enabled, if Compulsory
password renewal is also enabled in Advanced
Enforce password
Settings on the Basic Information tab page,
expiration
Compulsory password renewal in Advanced Settings
takes effect preferentially.
To ensure the security of all user accounts in the system,
setting user passwords to never expire is not supported.
If there are related service requirements, you can set a
single user's password to never expire as follows:
1. Choose System > Security Management > User
Management from the menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose Users. Click the
username of the desired user.
3. On the Basic Information tab, click Edit. In the
Advanced Settings area, enable Compulsory
password renewal and set Password validity (days)
to 0.
4. Click OK.
Force logout upon When a user password is reset, all online sessions
password reset generated by the user will be logged out.
Min. characters This parameter specifies the minimum number of
different between positions with a character different between the old and
new & old passwords new password.

Parameter Description
Password cannot be When setting a user password, the password must meet
an increasing, the following requirements:
decreasing, or
● The password cannot be digits or letters in ascending
interval sequence of
or descending sequence.
digits or letters
For example, the password cannot be abcdef, fedcba,
123456, or 654321.
● The password cannot contain an ascending or
descending arithmetic sequence in consecutive odd
positions or even positions.
For example, the password cannot be 1a2a3a,
5a3a1a, a2b2c2, or 2e2c2a.
Password cannot The password dictionary records weak password strings.
contain words in This policy is deselected by default.
password dictionary
● The password dictionary can be customized. For
example, abcd1234 is a weak password and if it is
added to the password dictionary, abcd1234 cannot
be used as a user password.
● In a password dictionary file, passwords are separated
by newline characters. In the password dictionary, a
password that contains more than 128 characters is
invalid.
If this policy item is selected, when you create a user and
set the password, the password cannot contain any
string in the password dictionary. Updating the password
dictionary will replace the existing password dictionary
in the system. The procedure is as follows:
1. Click Download to download the existing password
dictionary and modify it as required.
2. Click the folder icon and select the modified password
dictionary.
3. Click Upload to update the password dictionary.

Parameter Description
Convert strings in The string conversion rules define the rules for
password based on converting a character or string into an uppercase or
conversion rules lowercase letter. The escaped password must meet the
(refer to help requirements of the current password policy. The string
documentation to conversion rules file can contain multiple rules. Each rule
configure the rules) occupies a line and must meet the following
requirements:
NOTE
This parameter is ● In each rule, the strings on the left side of the last
displayed only when
equal sign (=) are converted to the first letter on the
Password cannot
right side. For example, |-|1234=a = x indicates that
contain words in
|-|1234=a is converted into x.
password dictionary
is selected. ● The spaces, newline characters, carriage returns, and
tab characters at the beginning and end of the strings
on the left and right sides of the last equal sign are
filtered out. For example, x=x = y indicates that x=x is
converted to y.
● The first character (after spaces, newline characters,
carriage returns, and tab characters are filtered out)
on the right side of the last equal sign must be a
letter. For example, x=x = 2ya is an invalid rule.
● If there are multiple rules with the same string on the
left side of the equal sign, they are executed in
sequence, so only the last rule takes effect.
If this policy item is selected, when a password contains
a string defined in the string conversion rules, the string
is converted into another string based on defined rules.
Updating the string conversion rules overwrites existing
rules, and invalid rules are filtered out. You can update
the rules as follows:
1. Click Download to download the existing string
conversion rules and modify them as required.
2. Click the folder icon and select the modified file.
3. Click Upload to update the string conversion rules.
Example: If a rule for converting the string |-|1234 a into
x exists in the string conversion rules, and xbcd!123 is
contained in the password dictionary, and when the
policy item is selected, |-|1234 abcd!123 cannot be used
as a user password.
Step 4 Click Apply.
----End
7.2.7.3 Disabling, Enabling, or Renaming the System Administrator User
The system administrator user is created during the installation of the PowerEcho.

user in CLI mode.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossadm users of the
management node.
● You have obtained the password for the system administrator user if you need
to rename the user.
Precautions
● After the system administrator user is disabled, the user cannot log in to the
PowerEcho. Exercise caution when performing this operation.
● After the system administrator is enabled, exercise caution when performing
operations as this user. This user has the highest permission on all resources
in the system. Do not perform any operations that hinder the system security.
For example, do not share or distribute the username and password of the
system administrator.
● After the system administrator user is renamed, this user needs to use the
new username to log in to the PowerEcho.
● After a default user is enabled, if the user is a local user, the user needs to
change the password upon the first login to the PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node, as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, perform operations on Management0 or Management1. For details about how to
obtain the IP address of a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a
Node.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: Password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Run the following command to go to the target directory:
> cd /opt/oss/manager/apps/MCCommonService/bin
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in single-server mode, and the management node and
product node are the same node, replace MCCommonService with UniEPLiteService. For
details about how to check that the management node and product node are the same
node, see 12.11 Checking the Deployment Mode of Nodes.
Step 4 Perform operations according to Table 7-20 based on site requirements.

Scenario Operation
Disabling the system 1. Run the following command to disable the
administrator user system administrator user:
> bash userTools.sh -cmd disableAdmin
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to disable the system administrator?
yes/no
2. Enter yes and press Enter.
If the following information is displayed, the
command execution is successful:
The script is executed successfully.
Enabling the system 1. Run the following command to enable the
administrator user system administrator user:
> bash userTools.sh -cmd enableAdmin
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to enable the system administrator?
yes/no
2. Enter yes and press Enter.
If the following information is displayed, the
command execution is successful:
The script is executed successfully.
Renaming the system 1. Run the following command to rename the
administrator user system administrator user:
> bash userTools.sh -cmd renameAdmin 'new
username'
NOTE
The new username must meet the following
requirements:
– Contain a maximum of 32 characters, and contain
characters no fewer than the number specified by
the Min. username length (characters)
parameter on the Account Policy page in System
> Security Management > User Policies on the
PowerEcho.
– Not contain escape characters, invisible characters,
special characters "&'+/;<=>?\©®`~*()|:,[]{} or
spaces.
– Be unique on the PowerEcho.
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to rename the system administrator?
yes/no
2. Enter yes and press Enter.
The following information is displayed:
Enter the password:
3. Enter the password for the system administrator
user and press Enter.
If the following information is displayed, the
command execution is successful:
The script is executed successfully.

----End
7.2.7.4 Disabling Two-Factor Authentication for a User
If verification codes cannot be obtained because the SMS server or email server is
abnormal, or if a user cannot log in to the PowerEcho, you can disable two-factor
authentication in CLI mode for the user to allow the user to normally log in to the
PowerEcho. If two-factor authentication is disabled for a user, the user does not
need to enter a mobile phone or email verification code when logging in to the
PowerEcho, which reduces the system security. Exercise caution when performing
this operation.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossadm users of the
management node.
● You have obtained the IP address of the management node.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node, as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, perform operations on Management0 or Management1. For details about how to
obtain the IP address of a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a
Node.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Run the following command to go to the target directory:
> cd /opt/oss/manager/apps/MCCommonService/bin
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in single-server mode, and the management node and
product node are the same node, replace MCCommonService with UniEPLiteService.
Step 4 Run the following command to disable two-factor authentication for a user:
> bash userTools.sh -cmd disableUser2FA -userName 'username'
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to disable two-factor authentication for user username? yes/no
Step 5 Enter yes and press Enter.
If information similar to the following is displayed, the command execution is
successful:
The script is executed successfully. Two-factor authentication of user username has been disabled.

You can modify parameters in CLI mode for the user management function
without redeploying this function.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossadm users of the
management node.
Context
With user management, you can set:
● Whether the username for login is case-sensitive.
● Whether local authentication is performed when remote authentication fails.
● Whether to implement suspension and lockout policies for remote users in
remote authentication mode.
● Whether to allow the operation of creating a user with the same username as
a deleted user.
● Number of deleted users to be saved.
● Whether remote users can change their passwords in Oracle LDAP server
remote authentication.
● Whether the TLS protocol of a specified earlier version can be selected when
the TLS secure connection is enabled in LDAP remote authentication.
● Whether to display the login status and enabling status columns, and whether
to display the All statuses option in the user list.
● Whether to enable the account lockout policy for the system administrator
user.
● Whether to enable the SM3 encryption algorithm (SM3 cryptographic hash
algorithm).
● Whether to encrypt user passwords using the HMAC_SM3 algorithm.
● Whether the system administrator needs to change the password upon the
next login to the web client of the PowerEcho.
● Whitelist of IP addresses for which the login verification is not displayed on
the web login page of the PowerEcho.
● Whether passwords can be transferred in ciphertext on the web client during
user login.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node, as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, perform operations on Management0 or Management1. For details about how to
obtain the IP address of a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a
Node.

Password: password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Run the following command to go to the target directory:
> cd /opt/oss/manager/apps/MCCommonService/bin
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in single-server mode, and the management node and
product node are the same node, replace MCCommonService with UniEPLiteService. For
details about how to check that the management node and product node are the same
node, see 12.11 Checking the Deployment Mode of Nodes.
Step 4 Optional: To require the system administrator to change the password upon the
next login to the web client of the PowerEcho, run the following command:
> bash userSettingTool.sh -cmd setAdminModifyPwdNextLogin
If the following information is displayed, the setting is successful, and the system
administrator needs to change the password when logging in to the web client of
the PowerEcho next time:
Execute the user setting tool successfully.
Step 5 Run the following command to query the settings of service parameters of user
management:
> bash userSettingTool.sh -cmd list
Step 6 Run the following command to modify the service parameters of user
management based on the query result and site requirements:
> bash userSettingTool.sh parameter value

7.2.7.6 Setting a Client IP Address Policy
A client IP address policy provides a mechanism for checking the accessibility of
the IP address used by an external access request. If existing client IP address
policies do not meet requirements, security administrators can set a client IP
address policy as required to specify the IP addresses used for logging in to the
PowerEcho, improving system security. The policy created in this section takes
effect only for the users with this policy.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Policies from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Client IP Address Policies.
Step 3 On the Client IP Address Policies page, click Create.
Step 4 Set a client IP address policy and click OK.

NO TICE
Exercise caution when you set the client IP address policies for the system
administrator user. If the client IP address of the system administrator user is not
within the bound IP address range, the user cannot log in again after logout.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
You can bind the policy to a user, and the policy takes effect only for the user. The
operations are as follows:
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Users.
Step 3 Click the username of the desired user.
Step 4 On the Access Policies tab page, click Edit and configure client IP address policies
for the user.
NO TE
Ensure that the IP address of your local PC is in the added IP address range.
----End
7.2.7.7 Setting Login Time Policies
A login time policy provides a control mechanism for checking the validity time of
an external access request during system operation. Security administrators set
login time policies as needed to specify the time period during which users are
allowed to log in to the PowerEcho, improving system security. The policy created
in this section takes effect only for the bound users.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Policies from the menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Login Time Policies.
Step 3 On the Login Time Policies page, click Create.
Step 4 Set the parameters of the login time policy and click OK.

NO TE
● The system administrator user is not restricted by the login time policy.
● If the start time is later than the end time for Effective hours, the system will
automatically set the end time to the next day. For example, if Effective hours is set to
16:00–15:00, login is allowed within 16:00–24:00 today and 0:00–15:00 the next day.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
You can bind the policy to a user, and the policy takes effect only for the user. The
operations are as follows:
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Management from the main
menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Users.
Step 3 Click the username of the desired user.
Step 4 On the Access Policies tab page, click Edit and configure login time policies for
the user.
----End
7.2.7.8 Setting Verification Code Policies
Security administrators can set verification code policies as required to improve
system access security. The verification policies apply to all users.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Security Management > User Policies from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Verification Code Policy.
Step 3 On the Verification Code Policy page, configure the policies.
Step 4 Click Apply.
----End
7.3 Configuring Users of the NetEco
Security management allows you to set parameters for the NetEco users to meet
function requirements in different scenarios.

To prevent users in idle state and those exposed to security risks, the system
allows you to enable, disable, and rename default users. After a default user is
disabled or renamed, the user cannot log in to the system. Perform the
subsequent step after confirming the risks.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the IP address of the node where SMLogLic resides. For
details about how to obtain the IP address of the node where a service
resides, see 12.3 Querying the Management IP Address of the Node Where
a Process or Service Resides.
● You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossuser users of the
node where SMLogLic resides.
Context
● Default users are those created upon the product installation and are
provided by user management. Default users cannot be deleted.
NO TE
When the management of local users is supported, the users who cannot be deleted
from the user list are default users. View the user list as follows:
1. Choose System > System Management > User Management from the main
menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose Users.
● If an online default user is disabled or renamed in CLI mode, the user will be
forcibly logged out.
● After a default user is enabled, if the user is a local user, the user needs to
change the password upon the first login to the NetEco.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the node where SMLogLic resides, as the sopuser user in
SSH mode.
NO TE
In a cluster system, perform operations in this section on a node where SMLogLic resides.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: password for the ossuser user
Step 3 Run the following command to go to the target directory, and perform Step 4 or
Step 5 as required:
cd /opt/oss/NetEco/apps/SMLogLicService/bin
Step 4 Run the corresponding command to disable or enable a default user.
1. Disable or enable a default user.

The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to disable the system administrator? yes/no
After the system administrator user is disabled, the user cannot log in to
the system. Exercise caution.
– Enabling the system administrator user
./userTools.sh -cmd enableAdmin
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to enable the system administrator? yes/no
The system administrator user has the highest permissions on all
resources in the system. Exercise caution when performing operations as
this user and do not perform any operations that hinder the system
security. For example, do not share or distribute the password for the
system administrator user.
– Disabling a default user (for example, testuser) other than the system
administrator user
./userTools.sh -cmd disableUser -name 'testuser'
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to disable user testuser? yes/no
After the default user is disabled, the user cannot log in to the system.
Exercise caution.
– Enabling a default user (for example, testuser) other than the system
administrator user
./userTools.sh -cmd enableUser -name 'testuser'
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to enable user testuser? yes/no
2. Enter yes and press Enter.
If information similar to the following is displayed, the command execution is
successful:
The script is executed successfully.
Step 5 Rename the default user.
NO TE
The new username must meet the following requirements:
● Contain a maximum of 32 characters, and contain characters no fewer than the number
specified by the corresponding parameter on the Account Policy page. Navigation path
to the Account Policy page: Choose from the main menu.
● Not contain escape characters, invisible characters, special characters "&'+/;<=>?
\©®`~*()|:,[]{} or spaces.
● Be different from existing usernames.
1. Run the following command to rename the default user:
– Renaming the system administrator user, for example, to newusername
./userTools.sh -cmd renameAdmin 'newusername'
The following information is displayed:

sessions of this user, and the user needs to use the new username to log
in to the system. Exercise caution when performing this operation.
– Renaming a default user other than the system administrator user, for
example, renaming the testuser user to newusername
./userTools.sh -cmd renameUser 'testuser' 'newusername'
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to rename the user testuser? yes/no
After the testuser user is renamed, the user needs to use the new
username to log in to the system, and online sessions of the testuser
user are automatically logged out. Confirm the risk before renaming the
user.
2. Enter yes, press Enter, and enter the user password as prompted.
Enter the password: password for the user to be renamed
If information similar to the following is displayed, the command execution is
successful.
The script is executed successfully.
----End
7.3.2 Disabling Two-Factor Authentication for a User
If two-factor authentication is enabled, a user may fail to log in to the system due
to the fault of the SMS server, email server, or other causes. The system allows
you to disable two-factor authentication for a user. If two-factor authentication is
disabled for a user, the user does not need to enter a mobile phone or email
verification code when logging in to the system, which reduces the system
security. Exercise caution when performing this operation.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossuser users for logging in
to the node where SMLogLic resides.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to a node where SMLogLic resides, as the sopuser user in SSH
mode. For details about how to obtain the IP address of the node where a service
resides, see 12.3 Querying the Management IP Address of the Node Where a
Process or Service Resides.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: password for the ossuser user
Step 3 Run the following command to switch to the target directory:
cd /opt/oss/NetEco/apps/SMLogLicService/bin
Step 4 Disable two-factor authentication.

user
./userTools.sh -cmd disableUser2FA -userName 'testuser'
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to disable two-factor authentication for user testuser? yes/no
If two-factor authentication is disabled for a user, the user does not need
to enter a mobile phone or email verification code when logging in to the
system, which reduces the system security. Exercise caution when
performing this operation.
– Disabling two-factor authentication for a user under a tenant, for
example, the testuser user under the testtenant tenant
./userTools.sh -cmd disableUser2FA -tenantName 'testtenant' -
userName 'testuser'
The following information is displayed:
Are you sure you want to disable two-factor authentication for user testuser? yes/no
If two-factor authentication is disabled for a user, the user does not need
to enter a mobile phone or email verification code when logging in to the
system, which reduces the system security. Exercise caution when
performing this operation.
2. Enter yes and press Enter.
If information similar to the following is displayed, the command execution is
successful:
The script is executed successfully. Two-factor authentication of user testuser has been disabled.
----End
7.3.3 Setting Service Parameters for User Management
To modify parameters for the user management function without redeploying this
function, you can run the userSettingTool.sh command script.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossuser users for logging in
to the node where SMLogLic resides.
Context
You can use userSettingTool.sh to set:
● Whether the username for login is case-sensitive.
● Whether local authentication is performed in remote authentication mode
when remote communication fails or connection to the remote server fails.
● Whether to implement suspension and lockout policies for remote users in
remote authentication mode.
● Whether to forbid the operation of creating a user with the same name as a
deleted user.
● Number of deleted users to be saved.
● Whether remote users can change their passwords in Oracle LDAP server

the TLS secure connection is enabled in LDAP remote authentication.
● Whether to display the login status and enabling status columns, and whether
to display the All statuses option in the user list.
● Whether to enable the account lockout policy for the system administrator
user.
● Whether to enable the SM3 encryption algorithm (SM3 cryptographic hash
algorithm).
● Whether to encrypt user passwords using the HMAC_SM3 algorithm.
● Whether the system administrator needs to change the password upon the
next login.
● Whitelist of IP addresses for which the login verification code is not displayed.
● Whether to enable certificate authentication for third-party users.
● Whether passwords can be transferred in ciphertext on the web client during
user login.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to a node where SMLogLic resides, as the sopuser user in SSH
mode. For details about how to obtain the IP address of the node where a service
resides, see 12.3 Querying the Management IP Address of the Node Where a
Process or Service Resides.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: Password for the ossuser user
Step 3 Run the following command to switch to the target directory:
cd /opt/oss/NetEco/apps/SMLogLicService/bin
Step 4 Optional: To force the system administrator to change the password upon the
next login, run the following command:
./userSettingTool.sh -cmd setAdminModifyPwdNextLogin
If information similar to the following is displayed, the setting is successful, and
the system administrator needs to change the password upon the next login:
Execute the user setting tool successfully.
Step 5 Run the following command to query the settings of service parameters for user
management:
./userSettingTool.sh -cmd list
If information similar to the following is displayed, the parameter settings are
successfully queried:
Setting Data:
Whether the user name for login is case-sensitive: 1
Whether to forbid the operation of creating a user with the same name as a deleted user: 0
Whether the local account lockout policy and IP address lockout policy are invalid for remote users in
remote authentication: 1

Whether a remote user can change their passwords in Oracle LDAP remote authentication: 0
The LDAP authentication scenario supports the following TLS protocols of a low version: [tlsv1.0]
Whether the userlist is simple for GMY: 0
Whitelist of IP addresses for which the login verification is not displayed: [10.10.10.10, 10.11.11.11]
Whether the account lockout policy for the admin user is enabled: 0

8
System Security Management
System security management plays an important part in preventing the system
from being attacked and protecting the system security. System security
management includes system certificate management, log management, and
other security policies.
8.1 Application Security (the PowerEcho)
Each service needs to register service configuration items with the application
security function. Security administrators determine whether to modify the
baselines as required. The application security function checks for risks of service
configuration items based on the baselines and provides risk warnings and
rectification suggestions, helping security administrators detect service security
risks and handle the problems in a timely manner.
Figure 8-1 shows the principles of the application security function.
Figure 8-1 Principles of the application security function
8.1.1 Managing Baselines
If the existing baselines do not meet service requirements, security administrators
can modify the risk levels and baselines (such as protocols, keys, or algorithms) of

settings.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 On the PowerEcho, choose System > Security Configuration Check >
Application Security from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Baseline Management.
Step 3 On the Baseline Management page, click the name of the desired service
configuration item to view the baseline of the service configuration item.
Step 4 Click Modify in the Operation column of the row that contains the desired service
configuration item to modify the baseline and change the risk level.
NO TE
● For service function security, you are advised to configure baselines of secure protocols,
keys, and algorithms for the service configuration items.
● Click Restore Defaults to restore the baseline of service configuration items to the
default settings.
Step 5 Click OK.
The application security function will check for risks of protocols, keys, and
algorithms used by service configuration items based on the configured baselines,
and provide risk warnings and rectification suggestions.
----End
8.1.2 Checking Service Configurations
Security administrators can configure check policies based on service security
requirements to periodically check whether protocols, keys, and algorithms used
by services meet the baselines, and rectify the risks based on the check results. To
meet special requirements such as compatibility requirements, you can configure
insecure protocols, keys, and algorithms on the panel for risk details. Exercise
caution when performing this operation. For service function security, you are
advised to use secure protocols, keys, and algorithms.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 On the PowerEcho, choose System > Security Configuration Check >

Step 3 On the Configuration Check page, check a single item or check items in batches
as required.
● Checking a single item
Click Check in the Operation column of the row that contains the required
item.
● Checking multiple items in batches
Click Check Now to check all items.
NO TE
Click Set Check Time and set the daily check time. The application security function
automatically checks service configuration items every day based on the set time.
Step 4 View check results.
● After the check is complete, view the results in the Check Result column.
● In the upper part of the Configuration Check page, view the result
distribution charts of all check items so that you can learn about the overall
risk information.
Step 5 Click the name of a required configuration item. On the panel that is displayed,
view the details about the risk. You can rectify the risk based on the suggestions.
Step 6 Optional: If Configuration Item can be set on the panel of risk details, set
Configuration Item based on site requirements and click Save.
NO TE
If the modified value contains insecure protocols, keys, or algorithms, security risks may
occur. Exercise caution when performing this operation. For service function security, you
are advised to use secure protocols, keys, and algorithms.
----End
Related Tasks
● Adding an item to the whitelist: If a service configuration item does not have
security risks or is not used, click Add to Whitelist in the Operation column
of the row that contains the item to add it to the whitelist. The application
security function will exclude whitelisted items when performing the security
check to improve the check efficiency.
● Removing an item from the whitelist: If the security check needs to include a
whitelist item, click Remove from Whitelist in the Operation column of the
row that contains the item.
8.2 Managing Keys
Keys are used to encrypt and decrypt sensitive data, such as passwords for
database users and certificates, to prevent the sensitive data from being tampered
with or stolen, ensuring data security. Key management includes updating keys
and configuring public key authentication.

The SSH key is dynamically generated during the installation of the PowerEcho
and is used to establish the trust relationship between the management node and
product nodes. To prevent security risks such as brute-force cracking of the SSH
access between the management node and product nodes, you need to update
the SSH key periodically (at least once every three years).
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Precautions
After the key is updated, historical OS backup files of the management node and
product nodes will become invalid. Therefore, the backup files generated before
the key update cannot be used for restoration. Back up data in a timely manner
after the key is updated.
Procedure
Step 1 On the PowerEcho, choose System > Certificate and Key > Update SSH Key from
the main menu.
Step 2 On the Update SSH Key page, perform operations as prompted. If the update
fails, contact technical support.
----End
8.3 Resetting the Protection Password
The protection password for the data integrity verification key is used for integrity
verification of backup data. The protection password for the data integrity
verification key is set during installation of the PowerEcho. You can determine
whether to use the protection password based on site requirements. You are
advised to use the protection password, or the security of backup data will be
reduced. If the protection password is used, change the protection password
periodically (at least once every three years) during routine maintenance. If
sensitive data is tampered with or stolen, you need to change the protection
password immediately.
Prerequisites
● You have logged in to the PowerEcho as a user attached to the
Administrators role. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the PowerEcho.
● You have obtained the current protection password for the data integrity
verification key of the system.
Precautions
● Keep the new protection password for the data integrity verification key

to enter the protection password for the data integrity verification key for
signature file verification. Otherwise, the PowerEcho cannot be restored.
● The protection password for the data integrity verification key must meet the
following complexity requirements:
– Contain 8 to 64 characters.
– Contain the following four types of characters:
▪
Uppercase letters
▪
Lowercase letters
▪
Digits
▪
Special characters ~@#^*-_+[{}]:./?%=! (Starting with ! is not
allowed.)
– Contain no more than two identical consecutive characters.
– Contain no more than three identical characters.
– Not be the same as the reverse of it, regardless of the letter case.
Procedure
Step 1 On the PowerEcho, choose System > Certificate and Key > Reset Protection
Password from the main menu.
Step 2 On the Reset Protection Password page, perform operations as prompted.
Table 8-1 Protection password operations
Scenario Operation Description
The Set New Password and The protection password can
protection Confirm Password, click improve the security of backup
password is Apply, and perform data. For example:
required. operations as prompted.

restoring the application and

see 10.6 Backing Up the PowerEcho and 10.5 Backing Up Products.
----End
8.4 Managing Certificates
Some communications of the PowerEcho and the NetEco (for example, the
communication between clients and the server, and that between services) use the
Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) mode and therefore require certificates. If a certificate
is about to expire or is cracked, update the certificate immediately. For system
security purposes, you need to update the certificates periodically.
8.4.1 Initial Certificate Risk Disclaimer
Huawei initial certificates are mandatory identity credentials for Huawei devices.
The use of initial certificates is as follows:
1. Huawei initial certificates are used only to establish initial security channels
for devices to connect to the customer network in the deployment phase.
2. The validity period of the Huawei initial certificate starts from the
manufacturing date. You can view the validity period of the initial certificate
on the System > About > Certificate Management > Service Certificate
Management page. The names and functions of the initial certificates are
described in Table 8-2.
3. After devices access the customer network, the management responsibilities
of the devices and digital certificates are transferred to the customer. Huawei
recommends that the customer replace the initial certificates used by devices
on the live network with customer's own certificates, and use certificates with
a short validity period and periodically replace the certificates in accordance
with industry best practices. This helps mitigate the risk of private key leakage
of digital certificates and prevent service interruption caused by certificate
expiration.
Table 8-2 Initial certificate
Usage Certificate Name
Certificate used by the app server NetEcoPhoneAppService-app
for the Android app client to access

devices
8.4.2 Certificate Overview
During the installation of the PowerEcho and the NetEco, SSL certificates are
imported or dynamically generated. After the initial installation of the PowerEcho
or the NetEco, apply for certificates from the CA, update the certificates in the
system. To prevent system security risks caused by brute force cracking or
certificate expiration, periodically update certificates.
Certificate Principles and Functions
The SSL certificate is a digital certificate based on the SSL protocol. It is similar to
the electronic copy of a driving license or passport and is used for identity
authentication between the client and the server. After the SSL encryption
mechanism is used, an encrypted communication channel is established between
the client and the server, which can ensure security and efficiency. Hyper Text
Transfer Protocol over Secure Socket Layer (HTTPS), a secure variant of HTTP, is
used for the internal communication of nodes or services of the PowerEcho and
the NetEco, communication between the PowerEcho or the NetEco and browsers
or other systems. The SSL protocol is the security foundation of HTTPS.

negotiating using a handshake protocol and transfer encrypted messages. A
single key encryption algorithm is used, such as Advanced Encryption
Standard (AES).
● Identity authentication: A public key encryption algorithm, such as Digital
Signature Standard (DSS), is used to add signatures to all the involved
communication parties.
● Data integrity: All messages transmitted during communication contain
digital signatures to ensure the message integrity. Digital signatures include
the message digest and message authentication code (MAC) generated by
the hash algorithm, such as secure hash algorithm (SHA).
Certificate Usage Scenarios
The SSL/HTTPS channel is used for internal network communication of NetEco
and network communication between NetEco and peripheral systems (that is, the
communication parties use the SSL certificate) to improve communication security
of the NetEco system, including the certificates for internal communication of
NetEco and the certificates for communication between NetEco and third-party
systems.
Certificate Update Methods
The methods for updating certificates of the PowerEcho and applicable scenarios
are as follows:
● GUI: Log in to the the PowerEcho client and update the certificate on the web
client.
– Update online: If the PowerEcho is interconnected with a certificate
authority (CA), you are advised to update certificates online on the web
client, so that you do not need to manually obtain and upload new
certificates and the operations are simple.
– Upload and update: If you have obtained a new certificate, you are
advised to manually upload it on the web client to update the certificate.
– Update certificates using CSR management: Certificate Signing Request
(CSR) management allows you to manage CSR files. To apply for a
certificate from a trusted CA, generate and export a CSR file in CSR
management. Then submit the CSR file to the CA to apply for a
certificate, and bind the certificate and its private key with the CSR file in
CSR management to update the certificate. A CSR file and its matching
private key are generated at the same time, which allows for a secure
and efficient way of updating certificates.
● Update in CLI mode: If you cannot log in to the PowerEcho web client due to
expiration of some certificates, you can only log in to the management node
and run commands to update the certificates.
The internal route (IR) forwards route requests between backend services. IR
certificates are used for mutual authentication during internal communication
and can be updated only in CLI mode. The IR certificates are authenticated by
the CA certificate. The IR certificates are updated when the CA certificate is

You can check certificate-related items during routine inspection to remind users
to update certificates in a timely manner to improve system security.
Step 1 On the PowerEcho, choose Maintenance > Operation and Maintenance
Management > Routine Inspection from the main menu.
Step 2 Click Inspect Health.
After the inspection is complete, you can click Export Report to save the report to
the local PC, decompress the report, and view the certificate-related check items
in the report.
----End

to improve security of internal and external communications. When an SSL
certificate expires or a specific certificate is required, replace the certificate.
