8.4.7 Preparing for File Upload-based Certificate Update
If you need to update a certificate by uploading certificate files using certificate
management, you need to generate a CSR and apply for the new certificate from
the CA in advance.
8.4.7.1 Generating a CSR File
When applying for a certificate from your trusted CA, you need to submit a
certificate signing request (CSR) to the CA. After obtaining the certificate, you
need to bind the certificate and its private key with the CSR so that the obtained
certificate can be used for update. The CSR is required when you update the
certificate by uploading certificate files. You can generate a CSR on the GUI or in
CLI mode. In CSR management, you can generate and modify CSRs, export CSRs
as files, and bind CSRs with certificates, which allows for a secure and efficient
way of updating certificates.
8.4.7.1.1 Generating a CSR File on the GUI
When CSRs are generated on the GUI, matching private keys are generated at the
same time. You do not need to export the private keys, which is more secure. You

● You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
● You have obtained the information about the CSR to be created, such as the
common name (CN), country or region (C), province or state (ST), city (L),
organization (O), department (OU), key algorithm, key length, subject
alternative name, certificate type, key usage, and extended key usage. Table
8-7 describes the key algorithms, key lengths, certificate types, key usages,
and extended key usages supported by the PowerEcho.
Table 8-7 Certificate information
Parameter Description
Key Key algorithm of the certificate.
algorithm
● RSA
● ECDSA
Key length ● Length of the RSA certificate key:
– 2048
– 3072
– 4096
– 8192
NOTE
RSA keys shorter than 2048 bits are insecure. For security
purposes, ensure that the private key of the certificate is an RSA
key of 3072 bits or longer.
● Length of the ECDSA certificate key:
– 256
– 384
NOTE
ECDSA keys shorter than 224 bits are insecure. For security
purposes, ensure that the private key of the certificate is an ECDSA
key of 256 bits or longer.
Certificate Type of the certificate.
type
● End Entity: Select this type if you do not need to use the
key corresponding to the certificate to issue certificates.
● CA: Select this type when you need to use the
corresponding key to issue certificates.

Parameter Description
Key usage Usage of the certificate key.
● Digital signature: Select this usage if the certificate
requires identity authentication and data integrity
authentication.
● Non-repudiation: Select this usage if the certificate
requires authentication on the digital signature of the
public key to prevent the signing entity from denying its
requests.
● Key encipherment: Select this usage if the certificate
requires an encryption protocol.
● Data encipherment: Select this usage if the certificate
encrypts its application data.
● Key agreement: Select this usage if the public key is used
by the sender and receiver to encrypt data during
communication through plaintext agreement.
● Certificate signing: Select this usage if the public key
authenticates the certificate signature. It can only be used
as a proxy certificate.
● CRL signing: Select this usage if the public key of the
certificate is used to verify the signature on the revocation
information.
● Encipher only: Select this usage if Key agreement is
selected. The public key is used only for encrypting data
during the key agreement.
● Decipher only: Select this usage if Key agreement is
selected. The public key is used only for decrypting data
during the key agreement.
Extended Extended usage of the certificate key.
key usage
● Server authentication: TLS WWW server authentication.
Select this usage when Digital signature, Key
encipherment, or Key agreement in Key usage is
selected.
● Client authentication: TLS WWW client authentication.
Select this usage when Digital signature or Key
agreement in Key usage is selected.
● Email protection: Email protection. Select this usage when
the Digital signature, Non-repudiation, Key
encipherment, or Key agreement in Key usage is
selected.
Subject Name used to indicate the certificate subject's name in other
alternative formats other than the subject name. The value can contain
name IP addresses, domain names, and email addresses.
(SAN)

A maximum of 20 CSRs can be managed.
Procedure
Step 1 On the PowerEcho, choose System > Certificate and Key > CSR Management
from the main menu.
Step 2 On the CSR Management page, click Generate CSR in the upper right corner.
Step 3 Configure the CSR parameters and perform operations as prompted.
Step 4 After a CSR is generated, click in the Operation column for the CSR to export
it as a file.
----End
Related Tasks
● Modifying a CSR: Click in the Operation column for the desired CSR.
● Deleting a CSR: If a CSR is no longer required or the number of CSRs has
reached the upper limit, click in the Operation column for the desired
CSR.
Follow-up Procedure
After you have obtained a certificate from the CA using the generated CSR, bind
the CSR and the certificate. For details, see 8.4.7.1.3 Binding a CSR with a
Certificate.
8.4.7.1.2 Generating a CSR File in CLI Mode
When applying for a certificate from your trusted CA, you need to submit a
certificate signing request (CSR) to the CA. This section describes how to generate
a CSR file in CLI mode.
Prerequisites
When you update certificates on the PowerEcho, the PowerEcho verifies the
password complexity of the private key file server_key.pem. The password must
meet the following requirements and you need to keep the password secure:
● Contain 10 to 32 characters.
● Be a combination of the following four types of characters:
– Uppercase letters
– Lowercase letters
– Digits
– Special characters, including !"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[]^`{_|}~
● Not contain double quotation marks (") and single quotation marks (') at the

● Contain less than four identical characters.
Precautions
● After obtaining a certificate file from a trusted CA using a CSR file, use the
certificate file with the private key file of the certificate.
● For security purposes, keep the generated private key file and protection
password secure.
Procedure
Step 1 Create the configuration file req.conf on your local PC and modify the
corresponding parameters. You need to edit the req_distinguished_name and
alt_names parts in the template file to meet the certificate application
requirements. Change the parameter values based on site requirements.
[req]
distinguished_name = req_distinguished_name
req_extensions = v3_req
prompt = no
[req_distinguished_name]
C = CN
ST = Guangdong
L = ShenZhen
O = xxx Technologies
OU = Development Department
CN = NetEco
emailAddress=xxx@xxx.com
[v3_req]
keyUsage = digitalSignature, keyEncipherment, dataEncipherment
subjectAltName = @alt_names
[alt_names]
DNS.1 = abcmaster.oss.net
IP.1 = 10.118.184.94
IP.2 = 10.118.184.177
● For details about the parameters in req_distinguished_name, see Table 8-8.
Table 8-8 Description of [req_distinguished_name] parameters
Parameter Description Example
C Country or region CN
code consisting of
two uppercase
letters. For
example, enter
CN for China.
ST Province/State Guangdong
L City ShenZhen
O Organization XXX Technologies
OU Department Development Department

Parameter Description Example
CN Common name of NetEco
a certificate
subject. It is
recommended
that the common
name contain a
unique code that
identifies a
product or non-
volatile product
information, such
as the ESN, IP
address, MAC
address, and
server name.
emailAddress Email address. 123@email.com
● [v3_req] specifies extended attributes of the certificate. For details about the
parameter description, see Table 8-9.
Table 8-9 Description of [v3_req] parameters
Parameter Description Example
keyUsage Key usage. The parameter digitalSignature,
value must contain keyEncipherment,
digitalSignature. dataEncipherment
subjectAltName Alternative subject name, @alt_names
which is used to indicate
the names in other
formats of the certificate
subject other than the
subject name. The value
can contain IP addresses
and domain names.
● If the CSR file need to be used for application for an ER certificate, configure
the IP address and domain name according to Table 8-10.
Among the [alt_names] parameters, at least one of the information items,
including the domain name and IP address (including those of the PowerEcho
and the NetEco) must be correct. Otherwise, the certificate you obtain will be
invalid. You are advised to use both the domain name and IP address for
application. Generally, this field does not need to be configured when request
files other than those for applying for ER certificates are generated. When this
field does not need to be configured, delete subjectAltName = @alt_names
and all lines following it.

Parameter Description Example
DNS.1 Domain name of the SubjectAltName field. abcmaster.ex
You can enter multiple domain names, which ample.net
are represented by DNS.1, DNS.2, and so on.
DNS.2 abcslave.exa
– You do not need to set this parameter
mple.net
when NetEco is accessible using only IP
addresses on browsers.
– You need to set this parameter to the
domain name when a DNS domain name
is used to access NetEco using a browser.
IP.1 IP address of the SubjectAltName parameter. 10.118.184.94
You can enter multiple IP addresses
IP.2 10.118.184.17
represented by IP.1, IP.2 and so on. Each IP
7
address occupies a line.
– If NetEco is deployed in single-server mode,
enter the client IP address of the
PowerEcho and the NetEco.
– If NetEco is deployed in cluster mode, enter
the floating IP address of the PowerEcho
and the NetEco.
Step 2 Generate a certificate request file and download it to the local PC:
1. Use FileZilla to upload the configuration file req.conf to a directory on any
management node, as the sopuser user in SFTP mode. For details, see 13.2
Transferring Files Using FileZilla. The following uses /home/sopuser as an
example.
2. Use PuTTY to log in to the node described in Step 2.1, as the sopuser user in
SSH mode.
3. Run the following commands to go to the path where the configuration file
req.conf is stored and generate the RSA key file:
cd /home/sopuser
openssl genrsa -out server_key.pem -aes256 3072
When the following information is displayed, enter the password of the RSA
key file based on the NetEco password complexity requirements:
...
Enter pass phrase for server_key.pem:
Verifying - Enter pass phrase for server_key.pem:
NO TE
RSA keys shorter than 2048 bits or ECDSA keys shorter than 224 bits are insecure. For
security purposes, ensure that the private key of the certificate is an RSA key of 3072
bits or longer or an ECDSA key of 256 bits or longer.
4. Run the following command to generate a certificate request file:

key file:
Enter pass phrase for server_key.pem:
5. Run the following command to view the certificate request file:
openssl req -text -noout -verify -in server.csr
6. Use FileZilla to download the key file server_key.pem and certificate request
file server.csr to your local PC.
Step 3 Run the following commands as the sopuser user and delete the temporary files:
cd /home/sopuser
rm -f req.conf
rm -f server.csr
rm -f server_key.pem
Step 4 After the certificate request file is generated, submit the application file to the CA.
After the CA issues the application file, you can obtain the corresponding identity
certificate.
----End
8.4.7.1.3 Binding a CSR with a Certificate
After you have obtained a certificate from the CA using a CSR generated on the
GUI, you need to bind the CSR with the certificate in CSR management, so that
you can obtain the certificate using CSR management during certificate update.
Prerequisites
● You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
● A CSR has been generated on the PowerEcho. For details, see 8.4.7.1.1
Generating a CSR File on the GUI.
● You have obtained a certificate from the CA.
– The total size of the certificate files cannot exceed 1 MB.
– The certificate file must be in .cer format.
Procedure
Step 1 On the PowerEcho, choose System > Certificate and Key > CSR Management
from the main menu.
Step 2 On the CSR Management page, click in the Operation column for the CSR
and perform operations as prompted.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
Certificates bound with CSRs can be used for updating ER certificates, syslog

about updating certificates by uploading certificate files.
8.4.7.2 Obtaininga Certificate from the Local CA
After applying for and obtaining a certificate using the Certificate Authority
Service, you can upload and update the certificate using certificate management.
If you apply for and obtain a certificate from another CA, skip this section.
Prerequisites
The Certificate Authority Service has been deployed on the NetEco.
Context
The following figure shows the process of applying for a certificate using the
Certificate Authority Service.
Figure 8-5 Process of applying for a certificate using the Certificate Authority
Service
8.4.7.2.1 Creating a CA
If you do not have a CA or have a CA that does not meet the requirements for
issuing certificates, you can configure one on the CA management page to
facilitate your subsequent certificate application and use.
Context
● When creating a CA, you need to set the associated profile and default profile.
You can manually create a certificate profile or use the predefined profile
provided by the system. The predefined profile cannot be deleted or modified.
Use a certificate profile whose key algorithm is RSA (3072-bit or longer key)
or ECDSA (256-bit or longer key) during upgrade. For details about the

NO TE
The default value of Validity offset for a preset certificate profile is -10 seconds,
indicating that the actual validity period of the certificate will be offset forward by 10
seconds when the preset certificate profile is used to apply for a certificate.
● Customizing an extension
On the Custom Extension page, click New. Set Name, OID, and Description
as required, and click Submit. The system displays a message indicating that
adding succeeded. Click OK. You can then use the added custom extension
when creating a certificate profile.
NO TE
● Name: name of the custom extension. Enter a string of 1 to 45 characters. Only
digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, underscores (_), and hyphens (-) are
allowed. The value cannot be null or all (case insensitive).
● OID: object identifier. If the custom extension is used in a certificate profile, the
OID is used as the unique identifier of the extension. The OID must contain 3 to
256 characters prefixed with 0., 1., or 2.. If the period (.) is followed by 0, 0 cannot
be followed by other digits. For example, 2.5.29.32.0 is in correct format, but 2.02
is in incorrect format.
● Description: Enter a string of 0 to 128 characters, including digits, uppercase
letters, lowercase letters, spaces, and punctuation marks (,.!:;?)(The punctuation
marks do not distinguish between Chinese and English, but the value cannot end
with ;).
● A maximum of 16 custom extensions can be added.
● If a custom extension has been associated with a certificate profile, you can modify
only the description but cannot delete the extension.
● If a custom extension is not associated with a certificate profile, you can modify
the OID and description and delete the extension.
Prerequisites
To publish the CRL corresponding to the CA to the CRL server, you need to add the
CRL server information to the system first. For details about the configuration
method, see Configuring a CRL Server (Optional).
Procedure
● Creating a certificate profile
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose PKI Management > Certificate Profile from the navigation tree
on the left.
c. Click New and set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-12.
A certificate profile contains information used for issuing certificates. If
extended items need to be configured for the issued certificate, see Table
8-13.

NO TE
● Critical: The certificate user performs strong verification on the extensions
that are marked as critical.
● Required: The extension value cannot be empty during certificate issuing.
● Permitted in request: he extension value in the request can be used during
certificate issuing.
If both Required and Permitted in request are selected, the extension value in
the request is used preferentially. If the request does not contain the extension
value, the default value in the certificate template is used. If only Permitted in
request is selected, the extension value in the request is used. If the request does
not contain the extension value, the issued certificate does not contain the
extension.
d. Click Submit.
You can view the created profile in the certificate profile list
NO TE
You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
● Creating a CA
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose PKI Management > CA from the navigation tree on the left.
c. Click New and set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-14.
NO TICE
When importing an external certificate, ensure that the certificate is
issued by a trusted CA. Otherwise, security risks exist.
d. Click Next.
e. In the profile association list, select the profile to be associated.
NO TE
● Configure an associated profile for the CA to issue certificates. The associated
profile must be a sub-CA profile or an end-entity profile.
● A CA must be associated with at least one profile, and a maximum of 16
profiles can be associated.
● You can select a preset certificate profile in the system or a certificate profile
customized in Creating a certificate profile.
f. In the default profile list, select an associated profile as the default
profile.
NO TE
● During certificate application using CMP, if the request carries the profile
name parameter, the specified profile is used; if the request does not carry the
profile name parameter, the default profile of the CA is used.

NO TE
● To issue a response protection identity certificate in the next step, select at
least one profile whose certificate level is End entity from the associate
profile list.
● If Certificate configuration mode is set to New CSR file, click Submit.
h. Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate and Issue response
protection identity certificate, and click Submit.
NO TE
● You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
● You can also click Previous to return to the previous page.
i. (Optional) If Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate is set to Yes in
Step 8, you need to determine whether to restart the service
immediately.
▪
In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Now, and then click OK to
make the configuration of Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate
take effect.
▪
In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Later. You can restart the
service on the Global Configuration > Service Management page.
NO TE
● The service is unavailable during the restart. Wait for several minutes and
perform other operations after the restart is complete.
● On the Global Configuration > Service Management page, you can view
the service status. If the service status changes from Restarting to Running,
the service is restarted.
● If the service is not restarted, the configuration of Set CA certificate to TLS
trust certificate does not take effect.
● After the CA is created, you can view the created CA in the CA list.
Related Tasks
Configuring a CRL Server (Optional)
If the CRL needs to be published to the FTP, LDAP, or SFTP server, you need to
configure the CRL server and associate the server with the CA. If the CRL does not
need to be published, you do not need to configure a CRL server.
1. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
2. Choose PKI Management > CRL from the navigation tree on the left.
3. On the CRL Server tab page, click New and set parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-15.
4. Click Submit.

Parameter Description Value
Validity Offset After the certificate is applied The value is a
for, the time difference negative integer
between the CA and the end ranging from -600 to
entity may exist. However, 0, in seconds.
the time difference may
Default value: -10
cause the certificate to not
Recommended value:
take effect, causing the
-10
certificate loading failure.
For example, if the current
time of the end entity is
12:10:15 and the current time
of the CA service is 12:10:20,
and the certificate effective
date is set to 12:10:20 (CA
service time), the certificate
has not taken effect on the
end entity after being applied
for, and the end entity may
fail to load the certificate.
The validity offset is used to
solve this problem. In the
preceding scenario, the
validity offset can be set to
-10 seconds. When the CA
issues a certificate, the
certificate effective date is
offset to -10 seconds (that is
12:10:15) from the current
server time. In this way, after
the certificate is issued, the
certificate takes effect on the
end entity.

RSA with a key shorter than

shorter than 224 bits are
insecure key algorithms. RSA
that uses a 2048-bit key size is a

longer key to generate the
private key of the certificate.

Path length constraint When the value of the path The path length

greater than 0, it indicates
When the certificate
the maximum number of
level of the profile is
intermediate subordinate CA
Subordinate CA, the
certificates that may exist in
default value of Path
the path from the CA
length constraint is
certificate to end entity
0.
certificates. If a CA system
has n layers, the path length
constraint of the top-layer CA
certificate is n - 2, and those
of the lower-layer certificates
is n - 3, n - 4, and so on. The
result is greater than or equal
to 0.
For example, if n is 4, the
four-layer structure of the CA
is root CA > subordinate CA1
> subordinate CA2 > end
entity certificate. That is, the
root CA issues the
subordinate CA1, subordinate
CA1 issues subordinate CA2,
and subordinate CA2 issues
the end entity certificate. In
this case, the path length of
the root CA is 2, the path
length of subordinate CA1 is
1, and the path length of
subordinate CA2 is 0.
NOTE
The path length constraint can
be set only when Certificate
level is set to Root CA or
Subordinate CA.

After a CA is created, you can configure CMP information, request verification,
response protection, and TLS to encrypt data when applying for a certificate using
CMP.
Prerequisites
A CA has been created.
Procedure
● Configuring CMP
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP from the navigation tree on the
left.
c. On the Protocol Configuration tab page, click Modify corresponding to
a CA. On the page that is displayed, set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-16.
d. Click Submit.
You can view the configured CMP protocol information in the protocol
configuration list.
NO TE
When applying for a certificate using CMP, you can configure CMP information,
requester, responder, and TLS as required to encrypt data.
● Applying for a certificate based on the CMP request URI
On the Protocol Configuration > CMP page, click the Protocol
Configuration tab. On this tab page, click a CA name, and copy the CMP
request URI corresponding to the CA for use. The CMP request URIs include
the following types:
– HTTP
▪
http://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name
▪
http://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name?certprofile=Certificate
profile name
– One-way authentication or Two-way authentication
▪
https://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name
▪
https://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name?certprofile=Certificate
profile name
For example, https://IP address:26802/cmp/caname?certprofile=profilename
indicates that a CMP request for applying for a certificate is sent to the CA
service through HTTPS. In the URL, the IP address indicates the IP address of
the CA server.

NO TE
● The certprofile parameter specifies the end entity profile used for issuing
certificates. This parameter is optional. If this parameter is not specified, the
default CA profile is used for certificate application.
● A CA may be associated with multiple profiles. Therefore, the CMP request URI
may have multiple values. Select a value based on the site requirements.
● The subject information in the certificate application request must be different
from that of the associated CA. Otherwise, certificate application fails.
● The port number can be 26801, 26802, or 26803. In the navigation pane, choose
Global Configuration > Port Management. On the Port Management page, you
can view or configure the protocol corresponding to the port number (HTTPS is
recommended because it is more secure than HTTP).
Related Tasks
● Configuring Request Verification
Requester is used to protect messages when an end entity sends a request to
the CA.
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP from the navigation tree on the
left.
c. On the Requester Configuration tab page, click New. On the Add
Requester Configuration page, set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-17.
d. Click Submit.
● Configuring Response Protection
You can configure response protection to encrypt the response messages sent
by the CA to the clients.
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP from the navigation tree on the
left.
c. On the Responder Configuration tab page, click New. On the Add
Responder Configuration page, set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-18.
d. Click Submit.
● Configuring TLS
When applying for a certificate using CMP, you can configure a TLS certificate
and TLS version to encrypt messages exchanged.
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose Global Configuration > TLS Configuration from the navigation
tree on the left.
c. Click Certificate Configuration. On the page that is displayed, set

For parameter descriptions for trust certificate chain, see Table Trust
certificate chain parameters.
▪
For parameter descriptions for identity certificate, see Table Identity
certificate parameters.
NO TE
● If the port has an effective CMP identity certificate, a message "The port
has an effective identity certificate. If the identity certificate is replaced
with the one issued by another CA, you need to update the TLS trust
certificate configured on the device. Otherwise, the device fails to
establish a TLS connection with the Certificate Authority Service. Confirm
the information before performing the operation." is displayed when the
user selects this port.
● If the TLS identity certificate of the privacy CA protocol already exists, a
dialog box is displayed when the user selects the privacy CA protocol.
● If you click OK, the current identity certificate will be replaced. If an
identity certificate issued by another CA is uploaded, the trust
certificate of the device must be updated accordingly. Otherwise,
the TLS connection between the device and the Certificate
Authority Service fails to be established.
● If you click Cancel, the identity certificate will not be replaced.
d. After the configuration is complete, click Submit.
NO TE
● When one-way TLS is selected, you only need to upload the identity
certificate. When two-way TLS is selected, you need to upload both the trust
certificate and identity certificate.
● After the trust certificate or identity certificate is successfully uploaded,
choose Global Configuration > Service Management in the navigation pane
and restart the corresponding service as prompted for the TLS configuration
to take effect.
Follow-up Procedure
● Downloading a CA certificate
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose PKI Management > CA from the navigation tree on the left.
c. Click a CA name.
d. Click Download CA Certificate to download the CA certificate to the
local computer.
NO TE
The downloaded request verification certificate is in .cer format.
● Downloading a request verification certificate
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP from the navigation tree on the

corresponding to the desired request verification to download the request
verification certificate.
NO TE
● The password is a string of 8 to 32 characters containing at least three of the
following: digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and special characters. In
addition, the password cannot contain two or more of the same characters
consecutively.
● The downloaded request verification certificate is in .p12 format. The
password is contained in the .p12 file. Enter the password for verification
when using a certificate file in .p12 format.
● You can download the request verification certificate only when the signature
type is self-signed.

8.4.7.2.3 Apply by Basic Info
To meet different requirements for certificate application, the Certificate Authority
Service allows you to apply for a certificate by entering basic information.

● On the Certificate Application page, the end entity certificate application
function is disabled by default and the Apply by Basic Info tab is not
displayed. To enable the end entity certificate application function, choose
Global Configuration > Function Management. In this case, the Apply by
Basic Info tab is displayed on the Certificate Application page.
● You have the permissions to view the application list, export the certificate
list, and apply for, update, export, download, view, revoke, and delete
certificates.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Certificate Application > Certificate Application from the navigation
tree on the left.
Step 3 On the Apply by Basic Info tab page, enter certificate application information.
NO TE
● The subject information in the certificate application request must be different from
that of the associated CA. Otherwise, certificate application fails.
● When applying for a certificate by configuring basic information, you can select only a
profile whose certificate level is End entity.
● The certificate validity period is the intersection of the certificate validity period of the
associated CA, the validity period set in the certificate profile, the maximum validity
period of the certificate that can be issued by the associated CA, and the current validity
period.
● You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
Step 4 Click Submit.
Step 5 Download a certificate.
● Download a file containing the certificate and the private key.
On the Certificate Application > Application List page, click Download
Certificate on the right of the certificate to download the certificate to the
local device.
NO TE
– When downloading a certificate that is requested by entering basic information,
you need to enter a password. The password is a string of 8 to 32 characters
containing at least three of the following: digits, uppercase letters, lowercase
letters, and special characters (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~ The input of the
aforementioned special characters is permitted, while other characters are not
allowed). In addition, the password cannot contain two or more of the same
characters consecutively.
– Only successfully obtained certificates can be downloaded.
● Download a certificate containing a public key file.
– On the Certificate Application > Application List page, click the on
the left, and click Download on the right of the certificate. For File type,
select Certificate, enter the File name of the certificate, and select File

right of the certificate. For File type, select Certificate, enter the File
name of the certificate, and select File format as needed. Click Submit
to download the certificate file.
● Download a private key file.
– On the Certificate Application > Application List page, click the on
the left, and click Download on the right of the certificate. For File type,
select Private key, enter the File name and File password of the private
key. Click Submit to download the private key file.
– On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Download on the
right of the certificate. For File type, select Private key, enter the File
name and File password of the private key. Click Submit to download
the private key file.
NO TE
● File types include Certificate, Private key, and Certificate and private key. The
corresponding file formats are DER(.cer)/PEM(.pem), PKCS#8(.pem), and
PKCS#12(.p12), respectively.
● The file name contains 1 to 20 case-insensitive characters, including digits, uppercase
letters, lowercase letters, underscores (_), and hyphens (-), but cannot be null or all.
● The password is a string of 8 to 32 characters containing at least three of the following:
digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and special characters (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?
@[\]^_`{|}~ The input of the aforementioned special characters is permitted, while other
characters are not allowed). In addition, the password cannot contain two or more of
the same characters consecutively.
● When downloading files in PKCS#12(.p12) or PKCS#8(.pem) format, you need to set the
file password. Keep the file password secure for future use.
● When you download a certificate in a specified file format, the file format that you can
select depends on the certificate application mode.
– Applying for a certificate based on basic information: You can download a file in
DER, PEM, PKCS#12, or PKCS#8 formats.
– Applying for a certificate by uploading a file: You can download a file in DER or
PEM format.
– Applying for a certificate using the privacy CA protocol: You can download a file in
DER or PEM format.
– Applying for a certificate using CMP: If the key pair is stored on the client, you can
download a file in DER or PEM format. If the key pair is stored on the CA server,
you can download a file in DER, PEM, PKCS#12, or PKCS#8 format.
----End
Related Tasks
● Viewing a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click the SN of a certificate to
view the certificate details.
NO TE
If the number of certificates issued by the Certificate Authority Service exceeds 80% of
the maximum number supported by the Certificate Authority Service, all expired
certificates are automatically deleted.

column of a certificate to update it. In the displayed dialog box, select New
effective time and New expiration time, and click Update. After the
certificate is updated, you can choose whether to download the certificate to
the local computer.
NO TE
● After the certificate is updated, a certificate is added to the certificate
management list. The SN, effective time, and expiration time of the new certificate
are different from those of the original certificate, but other information remains
the same.
● The certificate obtained by using CMP (with the key pair stored on the client),
privacy CA protocol or uploading the CSR file cannot be updated.
● Searching for a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, enter the subject name of the
certificate in the search box for a search. You can also specify the SN, validity
period, revocation reason, issuer or status of the certificate in Advanced
Search. The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by subject
name, SN, validity period or issuer.
On the Certificate Application > Application List page, enter the applicant,
CA name, subject or submitted time of the certificate in the search box for a
search. The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by applicant,
CA name, subject or submitted time.
● Revoking a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Revoke in the Operation
column of a certificate to revoke it.
NO TICE
Only certificates whose revocation reason is "Certificate is on hold" can have
their revocation canceled. Certificates revoked for other reasons can no longer
be trusted.
● Canceling certificate revocation
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Undo Revoke in the
Operation column of a certificate to cancel the certificate revocation.
● Exporting the certificate list
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Export List in the upper
right corner, set Start number, and click Submit to export the certificate list.
NO TE
A maximum of 50,000 data records can be exported at a time.
Example 1: If there are 60,000 certificates in the system, you can export them in two
times. Enter 1 and 50001 separately for Start number.
Example 2: If there are 30,000 certificates in the system, enter 1 for Start number to
export the list of all certificates at a time.

exported, click Export Certificates in the upper right corner, enter the file
name, and click Submit to export the selected certificates.
NO TE
A maximum of 100 certificates can be exported once. The exported file is in .zip
format, and the certificate file in the .zip package is in .pem format.
● Deleting a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, select one or more certificates
whose Expiration Time is the previous day (which must be based on UTC) or
earlier, and click Delete in the upper right corner.
In the Warning dialog box, click Export for Backup. In the Export
Certificates dialog box, enter the file name and click Submit to export the
certificates to be deleted to the local PC. After the system displays a message
indicating that the certificates are exported successfully, click OK.
NO TE
A maximum of 100 certificates can be deleted at a time. Deleted certificates cannot be
restored.
8.4.7.2.4 Apply by File Upload
To meet different requirements for certificate application, the Certificate Authority
Service allows you to apply for a certificate by uploading a CSR file.
Prerequisites
● The end entity certificate application function has been enabled Global
Configuration > Function Management.
● The CSR file has been obtained in advance.
● You have the permissions to view the application list, export the certificate
list, and apply for, update, export, download, view, revoke, and delete
certificates.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Certificate Application > Certificate Application from the navigation
tree on the left.
Step 3 On the Apply by File Upload tab page, enter certificate application information.
Step 4 Click next to CSR file.
Step 5 Select a local CSR file and click Submit.

NO TE
● The subject information in the CSR file must be different from that of the associated CA.
Otherwise, certificate application fails.
● The file to be uploaded must be in .csr, .txt, or .req format, and the size of a single file
cannot exceed 100 KB. The certificate file name is a string of 1 to 256 characters
containing Chinese characters, digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, underscores
(_), and hyphens (-), spaces, dots (.) and round brackets. It cannot start with a dots (.)
or space.
● If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA with a 1024-bit key
size, the certificate file has security risks. You are advised to upload a secure certificate
file. If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA with a 2048-bit
key size, the certificate file is of medium security. You need to confirm the operation in
the Information dialog box. You are advised to upload a secure certificate file.
● You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
Step 6 Download a certificate containing a public key file.
● On the Certificate Application > Application List page, click the on the
left, and click Download on the right of the certificate. For File type, select
Certificate, enter the File name of the certificate, and select File format as
needed. Click Submit to download the certificate file.
● On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Download on the right of
the certificate. For File type, select Certificate, enter the File name of the
certificate, and select File format as needed. Click Submit to download the
certificate file.
NO TE
● File types include Certificate, Private key, and Certificate and private key. The
corresponding file formats are DER(.cer)/PEM(.pem), PKCS#8(.pem), and
PKCS#12(.p12), respectively.
● The file name contains 1 to 20 case-insensitive characters, including digits, uppercase
letters, lowercase letters, underscores (_), and hyphens (-), but cannot be null or all.
● The password is a string of 8 to 32 characters containing at least three of the following:
digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and special characters (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?
@[\]^_`{|}~ The input of the aforementioned special characters is permitted, while other
characters are not allowed). In addition, the password cannot contain two or more of
the same characters consecutively.
● When downloading files in PKCS#12(.p12) or PKCS#8(.pem) format, you need to set the
file password. Keep the file password secure for future use.
● When you download a certificate in a specified file format, the file format that you can
select depends on the certificate application mode.
– Applying for a certificate based on basic information: You can download a file in
DER, PEM, PKCS#12, or PKCS#8 formats.
– Applying for a certificate by uploading a file: You can download a file in DER or
PEM format.
– Applying for a certificate using the privacy CA protocol: You can download a file in
DER or PEM format.
– Applying for a certificate using CMP: If the key pair is stored on the client, you can
download a file in DER or PEM format. If the key pair is stored on the CA server,
you can download a file in DER, PEM, PKCS#12, or PKCS#8 format.
----End

● Viewing a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click the SN of a certificate to
view the certificate details.

● Searching for a certificate

Operation column of a certificate to cancel the certificate revocation.
● Exporting the certificate list
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Export List in the upper

NO TE
A maximum of 50,000 data records can be exported at a time.
Example 1: If there are 60,000 certificates in the system, you can export them in two
times. Enter 1 and 50001 separately for Start number.
Example 2: If there are 30,000 certificates in the system, enter 1 for Start number to
export the list of all certificates at a time.
● Exporting certificates
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, select the certificates to be
exported, click Export Certificates in the upper right corner, enter the file
name, and click Submit to export the selected certificates.
NO TE
A maximum of 100 certificates can be exported once. The exported file is in .zip
format, and the certificate file in the .zip package is in .pem format.
● Deleting a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, select one or more certificates
whose Expiration Time is the previous day (which must be based on UTC) or
earlier, and click Delete in the upper right corner.
In the Warning dialog box, click Export for Backup. In the Export
Certificates dialog box, enter the file name and click Submit to export the
certificates to be deleted to the local PC. After the system displays a message
indicating that the certificates are exported successfully, click OK.
NO TE
A maximum of 100 certificates can be deleted at a time. Deleted certificates cannot be
restored.
8.4.8 Preparing for Updating Certificates Online by
Interconnecting with the CA Service
To update certificates or CRLs online, you need to interconnect the system with a
CA or CRL server first.
8.4.8.1 Creating a CA
If you do not have a CA or have a CA that does not meet the requirements for
issuing certificates, you can configure one on the CA management page to
facilitate your subsequent certificate application and use.
Context
● When creating a CA, you need to set the associated profile and default profile.
You can manually create a certificate profile or use the predefined profile
provided by the system. The predefined profile cannot be deleted or modified.
Use a certificate profile whose key algorithm is RSA (3072-bit or longer key)
or ECDSA (256-bit or longer key) during upgrade. For details about the
parameters of the predefined profile, see Table 8-21.

NO TE
The default value of Validity offset for a preset certificate profile is -10 seconds,
indicating that the actual validity period of the certificate will be offset forward by 10
seconds when the preset certificate profile is used to apply for a certificate.
● Customizing an extension
On the Custom Extension page, click New. Set Name, OID, and Description
as required, and click Submit. The system displays a message indicating that
adding succeeded. Click OK. You can then use the added custom extension
when creating a certificate profile.
NO TE
● Name: name of the custom extension. Enter a string of 1 to 45 characters. Only
digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, underscores (_), and hyphens (-) are
allowed. The value cannot be null or all (case insensitive).
● OID: object identifier. If the custom extension is used in a certificate profile, the
OID is used as the unique identifier of the extension. The OID must contain 3 to
256 characters prefixed with 0., 1., or 2.. If the period (.) is followed by 0, 0 cannot
be followed by other digits. For example, 2.5.29.32.0 is in correct format, but 2.02
is in incorrect format.
● Description: Enter a string of 0 to 128 characters, including digits, uppercase
letters, lowercase letters, spaces, and punctuation marks (,.!:;?)(The punctuation
marks do not distinguish between Chinese and English, but the value cannot end
with ;).
● A maximum of 16 custom extensions can be added.
● If a custom extension has been associated with a certificate profile, you can modify
only the description but cannot delete the extension.
● If a custom extension is not associated with a certificate profile, you can modify
the OID and description and delete the extension.
Prerequisites
To publish the CRL corresponding to the CA to the CRL server, you need to add the
CRL server information to the system first. For details about the configuration
method, see Configuring a CRL Server (Optional).
Procedure
● Creating a certificate profile
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose PKI Management > Certificate Profile from the navigation tree
on the left.
c. Click New and set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-22.
A certificate profile contains information used for issuing certificates. If
extended items need to be configured for the issued certificate, see Table
8-23.

NO TE
● Critical: The certificate user performs strong verification on the extensions
that are marked as critical.
● Required: The extension value cannot be empty during certificate issuing.
● Permitted in request: he extension value in the request can be used during
certificate issuing.
If both Required and Permitted in request are selected, the extension value in
the request is used preferentially. If the request does not contain the extension
value, the default value in the certificate template is used. If only Permitted in
request is selected, the extension value in the request is used. If the request does
not contain the extension value, the issued certificate does not contain the
extension.
d. Click Submit.
You can view the created profile in the certificate profile list
NO TE
You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
● Creating a CA
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
b. Choose PKI Management > CA from the navigation tree on the left.
c. Click New and set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-24.
NO TICE
When importing an external certificate, ensure that the certificate is
issued by a trusted CA. Otherwise, security risks exist.
d. Click Next.
e. In the profile association list, select the profile to be associated.
NO TE
● Configure an associated profile for the CA to issue certificates. The associated
profile must be a sub-CA profile or an end-entity profile.
● A CA must be associated with at least one profile, and a maximum of 16
profiles can be associated.
● You can select a preset certificate profile in the system or a certificate profile
customized in Creating a certificate profile.
f. In the default profile list, select an associated profile as the default
profile.
NO TE
● During certificate application using CMP, if the request carries the profile
name parameter, the specified profile is used; if the request does not carry the
profile name parameter, the default profile of the CA is used.

NO TE
● To issue a response protection identity certificate in the next step, select at
least one profile whose certificate level is End entity from the associate
profile list.
● If Certificate configuration mode is set to New CSR file, click Submit.
h. Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate and Issue response
protection identity certificate, and click Submit.
NO TE
● You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
● You can also click Previous to return to the previous page.
i. (Optional) If Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate is set to Yes in
Step 8, you need to determine whether to restart the service
immediately.
▪
In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Now, and then click OK to
make the configuration of Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate
take effect.
▪
In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Later. You can restart the
service on the Global Configuration > Service Management page.
NO TE
● The service is unavailable during the restart. Wait for several minutes and
perform other operations after the restart is complete.
● On the Global Configuration > Service Management page, you can view
the service status. If the service status changes from Restarting to Running,
the service is restarted.
● If the service is not restarted, the configuration of Set CA certificate to TLS
trust certificate does not take effect.
● After the CA is created, you can view the created CA in the CA list.
Related Tasks
Configuring a CRL Server (Optional)
If the CRL needs to be published to the FTP, LDAP, or SFTP server, you need to
configure the CRL server and associate the server with the CA. If the CRL does not
need to be published, you do not need to configure a CRL server.
1. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
2. Choose PKI Management > CRL from the navigation tree on the left.
3. On the CRL Server tab page, click New and set parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-25.
4. Click Submit.

Parameter Description Value
Validity Offset After the certificate is applied The value is a
for, the time difference negative integer
between the CA and the end ranging from -600 to
entity may exist. However, 0, in seconds.
the time difference may
Default value: -10
cause the certificate to not
Recommended value:
take effect, causing the
-10
certificate loading failure.
For example, if the current
time of the end entity is
12:10:15 and the current time
of the CA service is 12:10:20,
and the certificate effective
date is set to 12:10:20 (CA
service time), the certificate
has not taken effect on the
end entity after being applied
for, and the end entity may
fail to load the certificate.
The validity offset is used to
solve this problem. In the
preceding scenario, the
validity offset can be set to
-10 seconds. When the CA
issues a certificate, the
certificate effective date is
offset to -10 seconds (that is
12:10:15) from the current
server time. In this way, after
the certificate is issued, the
certificate takes effect on the
end entity.

NOTE secp384r1/P-384
RSA with a key shorter than
2048 bits and ECDSA with a key
shorter than 224 bits are
insecure key algorithms. RSA
that uses a 2048-bit key size is a
medium-strength algorithm. To
ensure system communication
security, you are advised to use
ECDSA with a 256-bit or longer
key or RSA with a 3072-bit or
longer key to generate the
private key of the certificate.

shorter than 224 bits are
insecure key algorithms. RSA

medium-strength algorithm. To
ensure system communication
security, you are advised to use
ECDSA with a 256-bit or longer

Parameter Description Value
Path length constraint When the value of the path The path length
length constraint extension is constraint must
0, it indicates that the CA range from 0 to 9.
certificate can only issue end
When the certificate
entity certificates in the valid
level of the profile is
certificate path. When the
Root CA, the default
value of the path length
value of Path length
constraint extension is
constraint is 1.
greater than 0, it indicates
When the certificate
the maximum number of
level of the profile is
intermediate subordinate CA
Subordinate CA, the
certificates that may exist in
default value of Path
the path from the CA
length constraint is
certificate to end entity
0.
certificates. If a CA system
has n layers, the path length
constraint of the top-layer CA
certificate is n - 2, and those
of the lower-layer certificates
is n - 3, n - 4, and so on. The
result is greater than or equal
to 0.
For example, if n is 4, the
four-layer structure of the CA
is root CA > subordinate CA1
> subordinate CA2 > end
entity certificate. That is, the
root CA issues the
subordinate CA1, subordinate
CA1 issues subordinate CA2,
and subordinate CA2 issues
the end entity certificate. In
this case, the path length of
the root CA is 2, the path
length of subordinate CA1 is
1, and the path length of
subordinate CA2 is 0.
NOTE
The path length constraint can
be set only when Certificate
level is set to Root CA or
Subordinate CA.

8.4.8.2.1 Setting CA Server Connection Parameters (the PowerEcho)
You can update certificates online by configuring parameters of a certificate
authority (CA) server to interconnect with the CA without manually importing
certificates. If you want to update certificates by uploading certificate files, skip
this section.
Concepts
Table 8-26 Signature algorithms
Signature Description
Algorithm
SHA256withRS SHA256 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
A (length: 256 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA384withRS SHA384 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
A (length: 384 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA512withRS SHA512 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
A (length: 512 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA256withRS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is more
ASSA-PSS secure than SHA256withRSA.
SHA384withRS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is more
ASSA-PSS secure than SHA384withRSA.
SHA512withRS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is more
ASSA-PSS secure than SHA512withRSA.
SHA256withEC SHA256 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
DSA (length: 256 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA384withEC SHA384 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
DSA (length: 384 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA512withEC SHA512 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
DSA (length: 512 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.

● You have the permissions to create, modify, query, and delete CA server
settings.
● Ensure that the time zone and time of the CA server are consistent with those
of the PowerEcho.
● You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
● Obtain the following information based on the CA interconnection mode:
– Local CA: The Certificate Authority Service has been deployed in the
NMS, and a CA is available in the Certificate Authority Service. For
details, see 8.4.7.2.1 Creating a CA.
NO TE
If the Certificate Authority Service is not deployed in the NMS, the local CA is not
displayed.
– External CA: You have obtained the certificate information in the local
configuration, such as the identity certificate, private key of the identity
certificate, private key password, and CA certificate chain.
Context
Currently, the system can be interconnected with an external CA server (such as
JIT and Ejbca) or an internal CA server (Certificate Authority Service). If the system
needs to interconnect with other CA servers, contact technical support to confirm
the interconnection availability.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Certificate and Key > CA Proxy Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose CA Server Connection from the navigation tree on the left.
Step 3 Click New and set parameters for connecting to a CA server as required. If the
internal CA service of the interconnected system is configured, select Local CA to
apply for a certificate using the internal communication mechanism. If the
external CA service of the interconnected system is configured, select External CA
to apply for a certificate from the CA using a standard protocol.
● When Connection mode is Local CA:
Select a CA that has been created in the Certificate Authority Service.
NO TE
A maximum of one local CA server connection configuration can be set for a CA
created in the Certificate Authority Service.
● When Connection mode is External CA, you need to enter basic information
and configure CMP and TLS.

Parameter Description
Name Configuration name of the CA server.
Protocol Selects CMP to connect to the CA. Currently, only CMPv2 is
supported.
CA address It consists of the protocol name (https or http) and the IP
address, port number, and address information of the CA
server. The format is as follows:
http(s): IP address: Port number/CA address information
Example value: https:IP address:26801/cmp/caname?
certprofileprofilename
NOTE
HTTPS is recommended, because it is more secure than HTTP.
CMP – If you have obtained the client identity certificate and
Configuration private key from the external CA, set CMP identity
certificate to Import identity certificate. For details
about the parameter suggestions, see Table 8-28.
– If you haven't obtained the client identity certificate and
private key from the external CA, set CMP identity
certificate to Apply for identity certificate via CSR.
For parameter suggestions, see Table 8-30.
NOTE
If Apply for identity certificate via CSR is selected, a CSR file
is generated in the CA proxy service, which is then used to
apply for an identity certificate from the external CA. This
identity certificate is finally imported to the CA Proxy Service
page.
TLS – If the CMP certificate cannot establish a TLS connection
Configuration with the CA service, obtain the identity certificate and
trust certificate chain files used for establishing the TLS
connection from the CA service. During TLS
configuration, set TLS Certificate mode to Import
certificate. For parameter suggestions, see Table 8-29.
– If the CMP certificate can establish a TLS connection
with the CA service, set TLS Certificate mode to Use
CMP certificate for TLS connection.
NOTE
If the CMP certificate is used to establish a TLS connection, the
CMP identity certificate is used as the TLS client identity
certificate, and the CA certificate chain is used as the TLS client
trust certificate chain. If the identity certificate is not issued by
the connected CA, the certificate chain must be uploaded.

Related Tasks
Table 8-31 Connection modes
Connect Operation Description
ion
mode
Local CA Modifying To modify settings of a CA server that has been
CA server configured, click Modify on the right of a CA server on
settings the CA Server Connection page.
NOTE
Modifying settings of a configured CA server may interrupt
services between the NetEco and the CA server. As a result,
users may not apply for certificates. Exercise caution when
performing this operation.
Deleting CA To delete settings of a CA server that has been
server configured, choose More > Delete on the right of a CA
settings server on the CA Server Connection page.
NOTE
Deleting settings of a configured CA server may interrupt
services between NetEco and the CA server. As a result, users
may not apply for certificates. Exercise caution when
performing this operation.
Detection On the CA Server Connection page, click Detection
Status Status on the right of a connected CA server and
check whether the server is running properly in the
Status column.
● Success: The connected CA server is valid.
● Failed: The connected CA server is unavailable. For
details about the failure, refer to the Failure
Details column.
External Downloadin After the CA server connection settings are complete,
CA g the CSR you can download the corresponding CSR file in the
file displayed dialog box, or by clicking Download CSR on
the right of a CA server on the CA Server Connection
page.
NOTE
If no CSR file is configured, you cannot download the CSR file
on the CA Server Connection page.

Connect Operation Description
ion
mode
Importing On the CA Server Connection page, click Import
an identity Identity Certificate on the right of a CA server to
certificate upload the corresponding identity certificate.
NOTE
● If no CSR file is configured, the identity certificate cannot
be imported on the CA Server Connection page.
● If the key algorithm of the certificate to be uploaded is
RSA and the key length is less than or equal to 1024, the
certificate file cannot be uploaded because of security
risks.
● If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key
is RSA with a 2048-bit key size, the certificate file is of
medium security. You need to confirm the operation in the
Information dialog box. You are advised to upload a
secure certificate file.
Modifying To modify settings of a CA server that has been
CA server configured, click Modify on the right of a CA server on
settings the CA Server Connection page.
NOTE
Modifying settings of a configured CA server may interrupt
services between the NetEco and the CA server. As a result,
users may not apply for certificates. Exercise caution when
performing this operation.
Deleting CA To delete settings of a CA server that has been
server configured, choose More > Delete on the right of a CA
settings server on the CA Server Connection page.
NOTE
Deleting settings of a configured CA server may interrupt
services between NetEco and the CA server. As a result, users
may not apply for certificates. Exercise caution when
performing this operation.
Updating On the CA Server Connection page, choose More >
the identity Update Identity Certificate on the right of a CA
certificate server to update the corresponding identity certificate.
Performing On the CA Server Connection page, choose More >
a Test Connectivity on the right of a CA server. The
connectivity connectivity status appears in the Status column.
test
8.4.8.2.2 Setting CA Server Connection Parameters (the NetEco)
This section describes how to set parameters for connecting the NetEco with a CA
server, which helps users apply for certificates from the connected CA server.

Table 8-32 Signature algorithms
Signature Description
Algorithm
SHA256withRS SHA256 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
A (length: 256 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA384withRS SHA384 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
A (length: 384 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA512withRS SHA512 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
A (length: 512 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA256withRS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is more
ASSA-PSS secure than SHA256withRSA.
SHA384withRS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is more
ASSA-PSS secure than SHA384withRSA.
SHA512withRS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is more
ASSA-PSS secure than SHA512withRSA.
SHA256withEC SHA256 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
DSA (length: 256 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA384withEC SHA384 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
DSA (length: 384 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
SHA512withEC SHA512 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash value
DSA (length: 512 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA is an
asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric encryption
on the hash value to obtain the final digital signature value.
Prerequisites
● You have the permissions to create, modify, query, and delete CA server
settings.
● You have obtained the CA server information, such as the IP address, port
number, and URL.

NMS, and a CA is available in the Certificate Authority Service. For
details, see 8.4.8.1 Creating a CA.
NO TE
If the Certificate Authority Service is not deployed in the NMS, the local CA is not
displayed.
– External CA: You have obtained the certificate information in the local
configuration, such as the identity certificate, private key of the identity
certificate, private key password, and CA certificate chain.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > CA Proxy Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose CA Server Connection from the navigation tree on the left.
Step 3 Click New and set parameters for connecting to a CA server as required. If the
internal CA service of the interconnected system is configured, select Local CA to
apply for a certificate using the internal communication mechanism. If the
external CA service of the interconnected system is configured, select External CA
to apply for a certificate from the CA using a standard protocol.
● When Connection mode is Local CA:
Select a CA that has been created in the Certificate Authority Service.
NO TE
A maximum of one local CA server connection configuration can be set for a CA
created in the Certificate Authority Service.
● When Connection mode is External CA, you need to enter basic information
and configure CMP and TLS.
Table 8-33 Configuration parameters
Parameter Description
Name Configuration name of the CA server.
Protocol Selects CMP to connect to the CA. Currently, only CMPv2 is
supported.
CA address It consists of the protocol name (https or http) and the IP
address, port number, and address information of the CA
server. The format is as follows:
http(s): IP address: Port number/CA address information
Example value: https:IP address:26801/cmp/caname?
certprofileprofilename
NOTE
HTTPS is recommended, because it is more secure than HTTP.

Parameter Description
CMP – If you have obtained the client identity certificate and
Configuration private key from the external CA, set CMP identity
certificate to Import identity certificate. For details
about the parameter suggestions, see Table 8-34.
– If you haven't obtained the client identity certificate and
private key from the external CA, set CMP identity
certificate to Apply for identity certificate via CSR.
For parameter suggestions, see Table 8-36.
NOTE
If Apply for identity certificate via CSR is selected, a CSR file
is generated in the CA proxy service, which is then used to
apply for an identity certificate from the external CA. This
identity certificate is finally imported to the CA Proxy Service
page.
TLS – If the CMP certificate cannot establish a TLS connection
Configuration with the CA service, obtain the identity certificate and
trust certificate chain files used for establishing the TLS
connection from the CA service. During TLS
configuration, set TLS Certificate mode to Import
certificate. For parameter suggestions, see Table 8-35.
– If the CMP certificate can establish a TLS connection
with the CA service, set TLS Certificate mode to Use
CMP certificate for TLS connection.
NOTE
If the CMP certificate is used to establish a TLS connection, the
CMP identity certificate is used as the TLS client identity
certificate, and the CA certificate chain is used as the TLS client
trust certificate chain. If the identity certificate is not issued by
the connected CA, the certificate chain must be uploaded.

8.4.8.2.3 Setting CRL Server Connection Parameters (the PowerEcho)
You can configure certificate revocation list (CRL) server parameters to
interconnect with a CRL server so that certificates can be revoked online without
manually importing the CRL file. If you want to update CRLs by uploading CRL

● You have the permissions to create, modify, query, and delete CRL server
settings.
● Obtain the corresponding information based on the CRL connection mode.
– Local CRL: The Certificate Authority Service has been deployed in the
NMS, and parameters for connecting to the local CA server have been
configured in the CA proxy service. For details, see 8.4.8.2.1 Setting CA
Server Connection Parameters (the PowerEcho).
NO TE
● After the parameters for connecting to the local CA server are configured, the
local CRL configuration record is automatically generated.
● After the upgrade, the local CA server may not have a local CRL configuration
record. In this case, you need to manually set the parameters for connecting
to the local CRL.
● If the Certificate Authority Service is not deployed in the NMS, the local CRL is
not displayed.
– External CRL: Information about the CRL server has been obtained, such
as the server address, server port number, user name, password
corresponding to the user name, and trust certificate used by the
verification server.
NO TE
The PowerEcho has been interconnected with a CA server. Ensure that the CA
server configuration (such as the IP address, port number, and address
information) is consistent with the CA server from which the certificate is to be
obtained. For details, see 8.4.8.2.1 Setting CA Server Connection Parameters
(the PowerEcho).
● Ensure that the time zone and time of the CA server and the CRL server are
consistent with those of the PowerEcho.
● You have logged in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the
PowerEcho.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > Certificate and Key > CA Proxy Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose CRL Server Connection from the navigation tree on the left.
Step 3 Click New and set CRL server connection parameters as needed.
● If the CRL connection mode is set to Local CRL:
Select a local CA server that has been created in the CA proxy service. A
maximum of one CA server connection configuration can be set for a CRL
server.

NO TE
Connect to the Certificate Authority Service. The following is an example:
1. The CA testlocalca has been created in the Certificate Authority Service.
2. A local CA connection record has been created on the CA Server Connection page
of the CA proxy service. (Configuration name for the local CA connection: test01;
corresponding CA name: testlocalca)
3. On the CRL Server Connection page, select Local CRL from the drop-down list
box. Then you can view the CRL server interconnection configuration (CA server
name: test01) automatically generated by the system. In this case, you can obtain
the CRL file corresponding to the testlocalca CA.
● If the CRL connection mode is set to External CRL:
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-38.

8.4.8.2.4 Setting CRL Server Connection Parameters (the NetEco)
This section describes how to set parameters for connecting the NetEco with a CRL
server, through which you can obtain CRL files from the CRL server.
Prerequisites
● You have the permissions to create, modify, query, and delete CRL server
settings.
● Obtain the corresponding information based on the CRL connection mode.
– Local CRL: The Certificate Authority Service has been deployed in the
NMS, and parameters for connecting to the local CA server have been
configured in the CA proxy service. For details, see 8.4.8.2.2 Setting CA

NO TE
● After the parameters for connecting to the local CA server are configured, the
local CRL configuration record is automatically generated.
● After the upgrade, the local CA server may not have a local CRL configuration
record. In this case, you need to manually set the parameters for connecting
to the local CRL.
● If the Certificate Authority Service is not deployed in the NMS, the local CRL is
not displayed.
– External CRL: Information about the CRL server has been obtained, such
as the server address, server port number, user name, password
corresponding to the user name, and trust certificate used by the
verification server.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > CA Proxy Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose CRL Server Connection from the navigation tree on the left.
Step 3 Click New and set CRL server connection parameters as needed.
● If the CRL connection mode is set to Local CRL:
Select a local CA server that has been created in the CA proxy service. A
maximum of one CA server connection configuration can be set for a CRL
server.
NO TE
Connect to the Certificate Authority Service. The following is an example:
1. The CA testlocalca has been created in the Certificate Authority Service.
2. A local CA connection record has been created on the CA Server Connection page
of the CA proxy service. (Configuration name for the local CA connection: test01;
corresponding CA name: testlocalca)
3. On the CRL Server Connection page, select Local CRL from the drop-down list
box. Then you can view the CRL server interconnection configuration (CA server
name: test01) automatically generated by the system. In this case, you can obtain
the CRL file corresponding to the testlocalca CA.
● If the CRL connection mode is set to External CRL:
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-40.

8.4.8.4 (Optional) Managing Certificate Templates (the NetEco)
The certificate template management function improves the efficiency of and
standardizes certificate update. You can create different certificate application
templates with common certificate information for different service scenarios.
When updating a certificate, you can select an existing template to simplify
certificate update operations and prevent repeated configuration of basic
certificate information for certificate update.

● You have the Query Certificate Application Template, Manage Certificate
Application Template, and Delete Certificate Application Template
permissions.
● You have obtained the certificate format, validity period, key algorithm, key
length, certificate type, key usage, and extended key usage from the
interconnected CA, ensuring that the planned certificate information is
correct.
● You have planned the information about the certificate, such as the certificate
format, common name (CN), country or region (C), province or state (ST),
city (L), organization (O), department (OU), email (E), validity period, key
algorithm, key length, subject alternative name, certificate type, key usage,
and extended key usage.
Context
● The validity period of a certificate applied from the CA is jointly determined
by the custom validity period, validity period of the CA root certificate,
maximum validity period supported by the CA, and validity period of the
application template supported by the CA. Generally, the validity period does
not exceed the maximum validity period supported by the CA and the validity
period of the application template supported by the CA. The actual validity
period depends on the interconnected CA.
● Table 8-43 describes the certificate formats, key algorithms, key lengths,
certificate types, key usages, and extended key usages supported by certificate
management.
Table 8-43 Certificate information
Parameter Description
Certificate Format of the certificate.
format
● PEM
● PKCS12
Key Key algorithm of the certificate.
algorithm
● RSA
● ECDSA

Parameter Description
Key length ● Length of the RSA certificate key:
– 8192
– 6144
– 4096
– 3072
– 2048
NOTE
RSA whose length is shorter than 2,048 bits is insecure. You are
advised to use the certificate encrypted by RSA whose length is
3,072 bits or longer.
● Length of the ECDSA certificate key:
– 521
– 384
– 256
Certificate Type of the certificate.
type
● End Entity: Select this type if you do not need to use the
key corresponding to the certificate to issue certificates.
● CA: Select this type when you need to use the
corresponding key to issue certificates.
