12.18 How Do I View Command Audit Logs?
This section describes how to query the command audit logs recorded in the
system.
Answer (EulerOS)
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node with logs to be viewed, as the
sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to obtain the IP address of a
node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a Node.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the root user:
> su - root
Password: password for the root user
Step 3 Run the following command to create a temporary directory for storing logs: In
the following operations, the log_info directory is used as an example. Replace it
based on site requirements.
# mkdir /tmp/log_info
Step 4 Run the following command to change the permissions for the log directory:
# chmod 750 /tmp/log_info
Step 5 Run the following commands to copy the OS logs to the temporary directory and
change the owner group and permission for the files:
# cp /var/log/messages /tmp/log_info
# chmod 640 /tmp/log_info/messages
# chown -h -R sopuser:sopgroup /tmp/log_info
# exit
Step 6 Use FileZilla to download the OS log files in the /tmp/log_info directory to a
directory on your local PC, as the sopuser user in SFTP mode. For details, see 13.2
Transferring Files Using FileZilla.
Step 7 After the logs are downloaded successfully, delete the temporary directory.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the management node in Step 1, as the sopuser user
in SSH mode.
2. Run the following commands to delete the temporary directory:
> cd /tmp
> rm -rf log_info

12.19 How Do I View Database Audit Logs
This section describes how to query the database audit logs recorded in the
system.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and root users of the database
node.
Procedure (Redis and GaussDB Databases)
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the node where the abnormal database instance resides as
the sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to obtain the IP address of
the node, see 12.4 Querying the Management IP Address of the Node Where a
Database Instance Resides
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the root user:
su - root
Password: password for the root user
Step 3 Run the following command to switch to the dbuser user:
su - dbuser
Step 4 Run the following commands to obtain the database instance name:
cd /opt/type/data/
ls -al
NO TE
Type indicates the database type, such as redis, and zenith.
Step 5 Run the following commands to view the log files:
● For the Redis database, run the following commands:
cd /opt/redis/data/database instance name
more database instance name-login.log
● GaussDB:
cd /opt/zenith/data/database instance name/log/audit
more zengine.aud or more zengine_times.aud
NO TE
The database instance name is obtained through Step 4.
----End

12.20 How Do I Prevent PuTTY from Being
Disconnected upon Timeout?
Question
How do I prevent PuTTY from being disconnected upon timeout when performing
operations on PuTTY?
Answer
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the desired node as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
Step 2 Run the following command to query the PuTTY timeout default value:
> export | grep TMOUT
If information similar to the following is displayed, PuTTY is automatically
disconnected if no operation is performed within 300 seconds:
declare -x TMOUT="300"
Step 3 Run the following command to set the PuTTY timeout period to 1 hour:
> export TMOUT=3600
Step 4 After the operation on PuTTY is complete, run the following command to restore
the PuTTY timeout period to the default value obtained in Step 2.
> export TMOUT=300
NO TE
If TMOUT is set to 0 or a negative value, the PuTTY session window does not automatically
exit. To ensure system security, you are advised to retain the default value.
----End
12.21 How Do I Check the Active/Standy Status of a
Node?
Question
How do I check the active/standby status of a node?
Answer
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm

where HyperHAAgent resides:
> cd /opt/oss/manager/apps/HyperHAAgent/bin
> bash hactl status resourcegroup
Information similar to the following is displayed. You can determine the working
status of the node based on the value of Group Role. The node in the online state
is active, and that in the offline state is standby. Obtain the IP address of the
desired node from the IP Address field.
Total: 2 Normal: 2 Abnormal: 0
Resource Group Name Cluster Name Node Group Role Health Status IP Address
Unfreeze in(s)
Group_node_level Cluster_0_1_node_level management0 online normal 10.10.10.11 0
Group_node_level Cluster_0_1_node_level management1 offline normal 10.10.10.12 0
----End
12.22 Obtaining the Public and Private Key Files of a
Backup Server
If you select the key authentication mode when configuring a backup server, you
need to upload the key file. This section describes how to obtain the public and
private key files of the backup server for configuring the backup server.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the password for the sopuser user of the management
node and the username of and password for the backup server user.
For a third-party backup server, you have obtained the passwords for the
login user, backup server user, and root user.
● Software that implements the standard SSH protocol (such as OpenSSH) has
been installed in the system for generating public and private key files.
Procedure
In this section, the management node is used as the backup server. If a third-party
backup server is used, replace the values as required.
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the backup server as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For
details, see 13.1 Logging In to a Server Using PuTTY.
NO TE
If a third-party backup server is used, perform the following operations as a user who has
the read, write, and execute permissions on the home directory of the backup server user
using SSHv2 or later.
Step 2 Run the following command to query the home directory of the backup server
user:

home directory of the backupuser user./home/omsubackup indicates the home
directory of the omsubackup user.
...
ossadm:x:3001:2000::/home/ossadm:/bin/bash
ossuser:x:3004:2000::/home/ossuser:/bin/bash
backupuser:x:4001:2000::/home/backupuser:/usr/libexec/openssh/sftp-server
...
Step 3 Run the following command to switch to the backup server user. If you fail to
switch to the backup server user, switch to the root user, and run the exit
command after completing the operations to exit the root user.
> su - Backup server user
Password: Password for the backup server user
Step 4 Run the following command to generate the public and private key files:
> ssh-keygen -a 100 -t ed25519 -f /Home directory of the backup server user/
id_ed25519
NO TE
In the preceding command, id_ed25519 is an example of the public and private key file
names. Replace it based on the site requirements. Ensure that no file with the same name
exists in the home directory of the backup server user to prevent file overwriting.
Information similar to the following is displayed. Set and confirm the password for
the public and private key files, and press Enter.
The public key file id_ed25519.pub and private key file id_ed25519 are generated
in the home directory of the backup server user.
Generating public/private ed25519 key pair.
Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):
Enter same passphrase again:
Your identification has been saved in /home/sopuser/id_ed25519.
Your public key has been saved in /home/sopuser/id_ed25519.pub.
NO TE
● The private key password must meet the following requirements:
– Contain 1 to 64 characters. It is recommended that the password contain 8 to 64
characters. Plan a password based on the server type.
– The password can only contain lowercase letters, uppercase letters, digits, and
special characters ~@#$%=^*-_+[{}]:./?! and cannot start with an exclamation
mark (!).
● Keep the private key file and private key password secure. In Backup and Restore >
Configuration > Configure Backup Parameters, if you select Key for Authentication
mode when configuring the backup server, the saved private key file and password are
required when you set Key file and Passphrase.
Step 5 Create the .ssh directory and file under the home directory of the backup server
user.
1. Run the following command to create the .ssh directory and file under the
home directory of the backup server user:
> mkdir /Home directory of the backup server user/.ssh
If the following information is displayed, the .ssh directory already exists. In

2. Run the following commands to create the file under the .ssh directory and
change the owner and permission for the directory and file:
> touch /Home directory of the backup server user/.ssh/authorized_keys
> chmod -R 700 /Home directory of the backup server user/.ssh
> chmod -R 600 /Home directory of the backup server user/.ssh/
authorized_keys
> chown -h -R Backup server user:Group of the backup server user/Home
directory of the backup server user/.ssh
Step 6 Run the following commands to copy the contents of the public key file to the
authorized_keys file:
> cd /Home directory of the backup server user
> cat id_ed25519.pub >> /Home directory of the backup server user/.ssh/
authorized_keys
> mv id_ed25519 id_ed25519.pem
Step 7 Use FileZilla to download the private key file id_ed25519.pem from the home
directory of the backup server user to the local PC in SFTP mode, as the user that
has logged in to the backup server.
Step 8 For security purposes, after the key authentication is complete, run the following
commands to delete the public and private key files and exit the backup server
user:
> cd /Home directory of the backup server user
> shred -u id_ed25519.pem id_ed25519.pub
> exit
----End
12.23 How Do I Switch Back to the Task Window After
PuTTY Is Disconnected?
After you log in to the NetEco server again on the PC, you need to switch back to
the task session window when PuTTY is disconnected.
Prerequisites
● The task is executed using the screen command, and not completed when
PuTTY is unexpectedly disconnected.
● You have logged in to the NetEco server as user sopuser in SSH mode. For
detailed operations, see 13.1 Logging In to a Server Using PuTTY.
Procedure
Step 1 Run the following command to switch to the root user:

Step 2 Run the following command to check whether the task is complete:
# screen -ls
The information similar to the following is displayed:
There is a screen on:
313550.install.sh (Detached)
NO TE
In the 313550.install.sh command output, 313550 indicates the process ID of the task and
install.sh indicates the task name.
If the command output contains the task script name, the task is being executed.
go to the step 3.
If No Sockets found in /var/run/screens/S -root is displayed, the task is
complete, view the log information of the script to check whether the task is
executed successfully.
# cd /opt/install/log
# tail -n 5 install.log
When the system displays information to the following, the installation is
successful:
Install successfully
Step 3 Run the following command to switch back to the task session window when
PuTTY is disconnected:
# screen -r "Task name"
If the information similar to the following is displayed, the task session window
has been opened by another PuTTY or the task has been completed:
There is no screen to be resumed matching install.sh.
----End
12.24 How Do I Configure Server Disks to Ensure that
Files Are Not Damaged When the Server Is Powered
Off Unexpectedly?
Server files may be damaged in the case of an unexpected power failure. This
section describes how to set server parameters so that files are not damaged in
the case of an unexpected power failure.

NO TE
Perform the following operations based on the iBMC version and RAID array card model:
● For the iBMC version, log in to the iBMC and view the iBMC version information on the
home page.
● To view the RAID array card model, log in to the iBMC and choose System > Storage
Management.
Procedure
● iBMC 3.XX
a. On the iBMC home page, choose Information from the main menu and
in the navigation pane on the left, choose System Info.
b. On the System Info page, select Storage > Configure.
c. Click next to Logical Drive.
d. Select Modify.
e. Select the logical disk to be configured.
f. Set Disk Cache Policy to Disable.
g. Click Save.
h. If there are multiple logical disks, repeat e to g to configure all logical
disks.
● iBMC 5.XX or later
– The RAID controller card model is 9440-8i, 9460-8i, or 3508.
i. On the iBMC home page, choose System > Storage Management.
ii. Click the logical disk name on the left.
iii. Click Settings on the right of the page.
iv. Set Drive Cache Policy to Disabled, and click Save.
v. If there are multiple logical disks, repeat ii to iv to configure all
logical disks.
– RAID controller card model is 3152.
i. On the iBMC main menu, choose System > BIOS Settings.
ii. Click Home. In the Virtual Console area, click Start and choose
HTML5 Integrated Remote Console (Private).
iii. Click the in the upper part of the iBMC management page and
select Forced Restart.
iv. During the restart, if information similar to Press Del go to Setup
Utility is displayed, press Del to enter the Basic Input Output System
(BIOS).
v. On the BIOS password input screen, enter the password and press
Enter. Press Enter when a message is displayed indicating that the
current password is the default one and needs to be changed.
NO TE
If the server has a default password, enter the default password
Admin@9000 before logging in to the BIOS. If you do not have the default

the Device Manager page, select the controller name Adaptec
SmartRAID3152-8i and press Enter.
vii. On the home page of the Adaptec SmartRAID 3152-8i controller,
choose Configure Controller Settings > Modify Controller Settings.
viii. Set Unconfigured Physical Drive Write Cache State, HBA Physical
Drive Write Cache State, and Configured Physical Drive Write
Cache State to Disabled.
ix. Choose [Submit Changes] > [Back to Main Menu] and press Enter.
12.25 Abnormal Service and Process Due to Damaged
ZookeeperService Data Files
Symptom
The CPU usage of the zookeeperapp process is above 100% for a long time.
MessagingBrokeService, DmqKafkaService, and MQAgent are abnormal.
ZookeeperService cannot be started and the ZookeeperService data files are
damaged.
Possible Causes
The data files of ZookeeperService are damaged due to unexpected power-off or
insufficient disk space on the node, causing the zookeeperapp process,
MessagingBrokeService, DmqKafkaService, and MQAgent to be abnormal.
Troubleshooting Procedure
Step 1 Start the zookeeperapp process.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the node where the abnormal zookeeperapp process
instance resides, as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to
obtain the IP address of the node where a process resides, see 12.3 Querying
the Management IP Address of the Node Where a Process or Service
Resides.
2. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
su - ossadm
Password: Password for the ossadm user
3. Run the following command to import environment variables:
source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh
4. Run the following command to query the status of ZookeeperService:
ipmc_adm -cmd statusapp -app ZookeeperService -tenant product name
– If information similar to the following is displayed and the service status
is STOPPED, the service is abnormal. Go to Step 1.6.
Display Name Process Type App Name Tenant Name Process Mode IP
PID Status
zookeeperapp-1-0 zookeeperapp ZookeeperService product name multi x.x.x.x

is RUNNING, the service is running. Go to Step 1.5.
Display Name Process Type App Name Tenant Name Process Mode IP
PID Status
zookeeperapp-1-0 zookeeperapp ZookeeperService product name multi x.x.x.x
66794 RUNNING
5. Run the following command to stop the zookeeperapp process:
ipmc_adm -cmd stopapp -tenant product name -app ZookeeperService
If information similar to the following is displayed, the zookeeperapp process
is stopped successfully. Otherwise, contact technical support.
Stopping process zookeeperapp-1-0 ... success
6. Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: Password for the ossuser user
7. Run the following command to view ZookeeperService logs:
less /opt/oss/log/product name/ZookeeperService/oss.zookeeper.log
If information similar to the following is displayed, the ZookeeperService files
are damaged. Otherwise, contact technical support.
java.io.IOException: Transaction log: /opt/share/oss/NetEco/ZookeeperService/datalog_v2/version-2/
log.c034b2 has invalid magic number -42140
Or
Unexpected exception, exiting abnormally java.io.IOException: Unreasonable length =
8. Delete damaged ZookeeperService files.
a. Run the following commands to check the owner and permission of the
files in datalog_v2:
cd /opt/share/oss/NetEco/ZookeeperService/datalog_v2
ll
Check whether the owner and permission of any file are ????.
▪
If yes, go to Step 1.8.b.
▪
If no, go to Step 1.8.c.
b. Run the following command to move the directory that contains files
whose owner and permission are ???? to another location. The version-2
directory in datalog_v2 is used as an example.
mv /opt/share/oss/NetEco/ZookeeperService/datalog_v2/version-2 /
version-2
c. Run the following commands to delete the files in datalog_v2:
cd /opt/share/oss/NetEco/ZookeeperService
rm -rf datalog_v2/*
d. Run the following commands to check the owner and permission of the
files in data_v2:
cd /opt/share/oss/NetEco/ZookeeperService/data_v2
ll
Check whether the owner and permission of any file are ????.

If no, go to Step 1.8.f.
e. Run the following command to move the directory that contains files
whose owner and permission are ???? to another location. The version-2
directory in data_v2 is used as an example.
mv /opt/share/oss/NetEco/ZookeeperService/data_v2/version-2 /
version-2
f. Run the following commands to delete the files in data_v2:
cd /opt/share/oss/NetEco/ZookeeperService
rm -rf data_v2/*
9. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
exit
10. Run the following command to import environment variables:
source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh
11. Run the following command to start the zookeeperapp process:
ipmc_adm -cmd startapp -tenant product name -app ZookeeperService
If information similar to the following is displayed, the zookeeperapp process
is started successfully. Otherwise, contact technical support.
Starting process zookeeperapp-1-0 ... success
12. Run the following command to switch back to the sopuser user:
exit
Step 2 Restart MessagingBrokeService.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the node where the msgbrksrv process instance
resides, as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to obtain the
IP address of the node where a process resides, see 12.3 Querying the
Management IP Address of the Node Where a Process or Service Resides.
NO TE
– If MessagingBrokeService is deployed on multiple nodes, perform Step 2.1 to Step
2.8 on each node.
– If MessagingBrokeService does not exist, go to 4.1.
2. Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: Password for the ossuser user
3. Run the following commands to delete the meta.properties file in the shared
directory of MessagingBrokeService:
cd /opt/share/oss/NetEco/MessagingBrokeService
rm meta.properties
4. Run the following command to switch back to the sopuser user:
exit
5. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
su - ossadm
Password: Password for the ossadm user

7. Run the following command to restart MessagingBrokeService:
ipmc_adm -cmd restartapp -tenant product name -app
MessagingBrokeService
If information similar to the following is displayed, MessagingBrokeService is
restarted successfully. Otherwise, contact technical support.
Stopping process msgbrksrv-0-0 ... success
8. Run the following command to switch back to the sopuser user:
exit
Step 3 Restart DmqKafkaService.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the node where the dmqkafkasvr process instance
resides, as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to obtain the
IP address of the node where a process resides, see 12.3 Querying the
Management IP Address of the Node Where a Process or Service Resides.
NO TE
– If DmqKafkaService is deployed on multiple nodes, perform Step 3.1 to Step 3.8
on each node.
– If DmqKafkaService does not exist, go to 4.1.
2. Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: Password for the ossuser user
3. Run the following commands to delete the meta.properties file in the shared
directory of DmqKafkaService:
cd /opt/share/oss/NetEco/DmqKafkaService/kafka-logs
rm meta.properties
4. Run the following command to switch back to the sopuser user:
exit
5. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
su - ossadm
Password: Password for the ossadm user
6. Run the following command to import environment variables:
source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh
7. Run the following command to restart DmqKafkaService:
ipmc_adm -cmd restartapp -tenant product name -app DmqKafkaService
If information similar to the following is displayed, DmqKafkaService is
restarted successfully. Otherwise, contact technical support.
Stopping process dmqkafkasvr-1-0 ... success
8. Run the following command to switch back to the sopuser user:
exit
Step 4 Restart MQAgent.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the node where the mqagent process instance resides,

Management IP Address of the Node Where a Process or Service Resides.
NO TE
If MQAgent is deployed on multiple nodes, perform 4.1 to 4.8 on each node.
2. Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: Password for the ossuser user
3. Run the following commands to delete the meta.properties file in the shared
directory of MQAgent:
cd /opt/share/oss/NetEco/MQAgent
rm -rf */data/kafka-logs/meta.properties
4. Run the following command to switch back to the sopuser user:
exit
5. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
su - ossadm
Password: Password for the ossadm user
6. Run the following command to import environment variables:
source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh
7. Run the following command to restart MQAgent:
ipmc_adm -cmd restartapp -tenant product name -app MQAgent
If information similar to the following is displayed, MQAgent is restarted
successfully. Otherwise, contact technical support.
Stopping process mqagent-1-0 ... success
8. Run the following command to switch back to the sopuser user:
exit
----End
12.26 Adding Domain Name Mappings
You need to add the mapping between an IP address and a domain name to your
local hosts file to access the IP address using the domain name. This section
describes the operations on Windows 10 as an example.
Procedure
Step 1 Access C:\windows\system32\drivers\etc on your local PC.
Step 2 Check whether you have all permissions for the hosts file.
1. Right-click the hosts file.
2. Choose Properties.
3. In the hosts Properties window, click the Security tab and click your account
in the Group and user names area.

– If no, go to Step 2.5.
5. Click Edit. In the Permissions for hosts window, click your account, and select
Allow for Full control.
6. Click Apply. In the dialog box that is displayed, click Yes.
7. Click OK, and click OK.
Step 3 Open the hosts file using a text editor.
Step 4 Add the desired domain name mapping to the file, for example, 10.10.10.10
test.com, and save the file. Replace the IP address and domain name based on
site requirements.
...
# localhost name resolution is handled within DNS itself.
# 127.0.0.1 localhost
# ::1 localhost
10.10.10.10 test.com
Step 5 Verify the domain name mapping.
1. Press Windows logo key+R, enter cmd, and click OK.
2. Run the following command to verify the domain name mapping:
ping test.com
If information similar to the following is displayed, the mapping is added
successfully. Otherwise, contact technical support.
Pinging test.com [10.10.10.10] with 32 bytes of data:
Reply from 10.3.42.32: bytes=32 time=1ms TTL=57
...
Ping statistics for 10.10.10.10:
Packets: Sent = 4, Received = 4, Lost = 0 (0% loss),
Approximate round trip times in milli-seconds:
Minimum = 1ms, Maximum = 2ms, Average = 1ms
----End

13
Common Operations
This chapter describes common operations performed on browsers.
13.1 Logging In to a Server Using PuTTY
After the OS is installed, you can use PuTTY to log in to a server in SSH mode.
Obtain PuTTY 0.75 or later.
Prerequisites
You have obtained PuTTY of the latest version from https://
www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/ and have installed it on your
local PC.
Procedure
The following uses PuTTY 0.75 as an example:
Step 1 Run PuTTY and click Session.
Step 2 In the Host Name (or IP address) text box, enter the IP address of the node that
you want to log in to.
NO TE
Use the fixed IP address of the node for login. The floating IP address cannot be used for
login.
Step 3 In the Connection type area, select SSH
Step 4 In the Close window on exit area, select Only on clean exit.
Step 5 Choose Window > Translation from the navigation tree.
Step 6 Set Remote character set to UTF-8.
Step 7 Click Open. If this is the first time you are using PuTTY, the PuTTY Security Alert
dialog box will be displayed. Click Accept.
Step 8 When the following information is displayed, enter a username and press Enter:

NO TE
After security hardening is performed on the OS, you need to log in to the node as a user
with the SSH permission (for example, the sopuser user) and run the su command to
switch to another user. Ensure that you run the su command from the /bin directory or
directly run the /bin/su - User to be switched to command to switch to another user.
Step 9 When the following information is displayed, enter the user password and press
Enter:
Username@IP address's password:
NO TE
For security purposes, change the password periodically and keep the new password secure.
----End
13.2 Transferring Files Using FileZilla
This section describes how to use FileZilla to transfer files. This section uses
FileZilla 3.58.0 as an example.
Prerequisites
You have obtained FileZilla of the latest version from https://filezilla-project.org
and have installed it on your PC.
NO TE
Obtain FileZilla 3.58.0 or later.
Procedure
Step 1 Start FileZilla.
Step 2 Configure the parameters according to Table 13-1.
Table 13-1 Description
Parameter Description
Host IP address of the server.
NOTE
If the IP address is an IPv6 address, enclose it with square brackets, for
example, [IPv6 address of the server].
Username Enter the username and password of the server. The user has
permission to access the destination directory.
Password
NOTE
After the OS security hardening is performed, you cannot log in to the
server as the root user in SFTP mode. Instead, you can log in to the server
only as a user with SFTP access permission, for example, the sopuser user.
Port 22

Step 4 In the Unknown host key dialog box, select Always trust this host, add this key
to the cache and click OK.
Step 5 In the Remote site area, set the destination directory for uploading or
downloading files.
After you set the directory, the Remote site area displays all files stored in this
directory.
Step 6 In the Local site area, set the source directory on the PC for uploading or
downloading files.
After you set the directory, the Local site area displays all files stored in this
directory.
Step 7 Perform the following operations as required.
If You Need to... Then...
Upload files In the Local site area, right-click the file to be
uploaded on the PC and choose Upload from the
shortcut menu.
Download files In the Remote site area, right-click the file to be
downloaded to the PC and choose Download from the
shortcut menu.
NO TE
You can click the Successful transfers or Failed transfers tab to view the operation
process. If the upload or download fails, click the Failed transfers tab in the lower left area
of the FileZilla window. Then right-click the file that fails to be transferred and choose
Reset and requeue selected files from the shortcut menu to resume the file transfer.
----End
Related Tasks
NO TE
● The user for logging in to the node must be sopuser.
● The space of the directory may be limited or the system may periodically clear
temporary files. Therefore, you are advised to upload and download files within one day.
If the files are missing before your operation is complete, the system may have
automatically deleted the files. You are advised to upload the files again.
● After the operation is complete, delete the files uploaded to the directory to prevent the
files from being stolen.
● Use FileZilla to upload a local file to the target directory on a node.
For example, upload the aaa.txt file from your local PC to on node A.
a. Use FileZilla to upload the aaa.txt file to on node A, as the sopuser user
in SFTP mode.
b. Use PuTTY to log in to node A as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For

aaa.txt file in the directory:
> cd
> rm -f aaa.txt
● Use FileZilla to download a file from the source directory on a node to your
local PC.
For example, download the aaa.txt file from the directory on node A to your
local PC.
a. Use FileZilla to download the aaa.txt file from the directory on node A to
your local PC, as the sopuser user in SFTP mode.
b. After downloading the file, run the following commands to delete the
aaa.txt file in the directory:
> cd
> rm -f aaa.txt
13.3 Encrypting the Private Key of the Signature
Certificate (the NetEco)
When you update a certificate, the encrypted private key of the signature
certificate is required. This section describes how to encrypt the private key.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the signature certificate's private key signing_key.pem
that is to be encrypted.
● You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossuser users for
logging in to the node where the service that requires private key encryption
resides.
Procedure
Step 1 Use FileZilla to upload the signing_key.pem file to the directory on the node
where the file to be encrypted resides, as the sopuser user in SFTP mode. For
details, see 13.2 Transferring Files Using FileZilla.
Step 2 Use PuTTY to log in to the node where the service that requires private key
encryption resides, as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to
obtain the IP address of the node, see 12.3 Querying the Management IP
Address of the Node Where a Process or Service Resides.
Step 3 Run the following command to set the permission for the file:
chmod -R 640 /signing_key.pem
Step 4 Run the following command to switch to the ossuser user:
su - ossuser
Password: password for the ossuser user
Step 5 Run the following command to copy the file:

cd /home/ossuser
chown ossuser:ossgroup signing_key.pem
chmod 600 signing_key.pem
Step 7 Run the following command to encrypt the private key:
openssl rsa -in signing_key.pem -aes128 -out signing_key.pem
Enter the new password as prompted.
Enter PEM pass phrase: Password for signing_key.pem
Verifying - Enter PEM pass phrase: Password for signing_key.pem
NO TE
● For security purposes, you are advised to use a secure encryption algorithm to encrypt
the private key.
● The encrypted signing_key.pem file overwrites the unencrypted signing_key.pem file
in /home/ossuser.
Step 8 Run the following command to switch to the sopuser user:
exit
Step 9 Run the following command to delete the temporary file:
cd
rm -rf signing_key.pem
----End
13.4 Encrypting the Private Key of the Signature
Certificate (the PowerEcho)
Before updating the certificate, obtain the private key file of the signature
certificate. The private key must be encrypted using the AES-128-CBC algorithm.
This section describes how to encrypt the private key and uses the user
management certificate as the example. This section describes how to encrypt the
private key.
Prerequisites
● You have generated or purchased the following files: signing_cert.pem,
ca.pem, and signing_key.pem.
– signing_cert.pem: public key of the signature certificate. The following
configurations are supported when the signing_cert.pem file is
generated:
▪
You can determine whether to configure keyUsage and
extendedKeyUsage in the configuration file.
▪
If only keyUsage is configured, its value must contain Digital

If both keyUsage and extendedKeyUsage are configured, the value
of keyUsage must contain Digital Signature and Key
Encipherment, and the value of extendedKeyUsage must contain
the Secure Email Object Identifier (OID) 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.4.
– signing_key.pem: private key of the signature certificate.
– ca.pem: trust certificate of the CA.
● You have obtained the password for the sopuser user for logging in to the
node where the service that requires private key encryption resides.
Procedure
Step 1 Use FileZilla to upload the signing_key.pem file to the /home/sopuser directory
on the management node, as the sopuser user in SFTP mode. For details, see 13.2
Transferring Files Using FileZilla.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, perform operations on Management0 and then on Management1. For details about
how to obtain the IP address of a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address
of a Node.
Step 2 Use PuTTY to log in to the node described in Step 1, as the sopuser user in SSH
mode.
Step 3 Run the following command to set the permission for the signing_key.pem file:
> chmod 600 /home/sopuser/signing_key.pem
Step 4 Run the following command to encrypt the private key:
> openssl rsa -in signing_key.pem -aes256 -out signing_key.pem
Enter the password as prompted.
Enter PEM pass phrase: password for signing_key.pem
Verifying - Enter PEM pass phrase: password for signing_key.pem
NO TE
The encrypted signing_key.pem file overwrites the unencrypted signing_key.pem file in
the current directory.
Step 5 Use FileZilla to download the signing_key.pem file in the /home/sopuser
directory to your local PC, as the sopuser user in SFTP mode.
Step 6 Use PuTTY to run the following commands as the sopuser user to delete
temporary files:
> cd /home/sopuser
> rm -f signing_key.pem
----End

13.5 How Do I Change the Database Instance
Password?
If the password of a database instance fails to be changed when you change the
database passwords of the PowerEcho in batches, and the password of the
instance cannot be changed on the GUI, you can change the password in CLI
mode.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossadm users of the
PowerEcho node.
● You have obtained information about the database user whose password is to
be changed, such as the database instance name, database type, and
database user name. To obtain such information, on the PowerEcho, choose
System > Log Mangement > Operation Logs from the main menu and view
the operation log details.
● You have obtained the old and new passwords of the database user whose
password is to be changed.
● All services of the product have been stopped. For details, see 4.2 Stopping
Product Services.
● Ensure that services of the database with the database user password to be
changed are running properly. For details, see 5.1.4 Monitoring Databases.
Context
The user password must meet the following password complexity requirements:
● The password must contain 8 to 64 characters.
● The password must be a combination of the following four types of
characters:
– Uppercase letters
– Lowercase letters
– Digits
– Special characters ~ @ # ^ * - _ + [ { } ] : . / ?
● The password cannot contain more than two consecutive identical characters.
● The same character can be used three times at most.
● The password must contain at least two different characters from the initial
password.
● The password cannot contain the username or the reverse of it, regardless of
the letter case.
NO TE
● For the GaussDB database, the new password must meet the complexity
requirements and contain at least two different characters from the old password.
The new password cannot be the same as any used in the past 60 days, and

Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the PowerEcho node as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple the PowerEcho
nodes, perform operations only on Management0. For details about how to obtain the IP
address of a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a Node
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Run the following commands to change the database user password:
> cd /opt/oss/manager/agent/MaintenanceService/tools/sysmt
> bash modifydbpad.sh -pn all -dbtype "database type" -inst database instance
name -u database username [-startProduct true]
NO TE
● database type indicates the type of the database user to be changed, for example,
GaussDB T V3 or Redis. If the database type name contains spaces, for example,
GaussDB T V3, add double quotation marks (") to the database type when running the
command.
● database instance name indicates the name of the database instance to be changed. If
the database has master and slave database instances, enter the master database
instance ID.
To view the master database instance ID, log in to the PowerEcho and perform the
following steps:
1. On the PowerEcho, choose Maintenance > Operation and Maintenance
Management > Panoramic Monitoring from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose Middleware Monitoring.
3. In the upper left corner of the Node Monitoring page, select the product
4. Click the Relational Databases tab.
● database username indicates the name of the database user with password to be
changed.
● If the startProduct parameter is set to true, the product services will be automatically
restarted after the database user password is changed. If the parameter is set to false,
the product services will not be automatically started.
The following information is displayed. Enter the old password, new password, and
confirm the new password.
Old Password:
New Password:
Retype New Password:
The product information is displayed. Enter the ID of the product whose database
password needs to be changed.
1.productDesc:product alias productName:product name productType:product type
productNum: 1
If the following information is displayed, the database instance password is

----End
13.6 How Do I Query the Version Number of the
PowerEcho?
Question
This section describes how to query the version number of the PowerEcho on
browser.
Answer
Step 1 Log in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the PowerEcho.
Step 2 On the PowerEcho, choose System > About from the main menu, or click in
the upper right corner of a page.
Step 3 In the dialog box that is displayed, view the version number of the PowerEcho.
----End
13.7 Checking the Status of the PowerEcho Service
This section describes how to check the running status of the PowerEcho service
by running commands.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossadm users of the
management node.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
For details, see 13.1 Logging In to a Server Using PuTTY.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, perform operations on Management0 and then on Management1. For details about
how to obtain the IP address of a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address
of a Node.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Check the running status of the PowerEcho.

Information similar to the following is displayed:
Display Name Process Type App Name Tenant Name Process Mode IP
PID Status
backupwebsite-0-0 backupwebsite BackupWebsite manager cluster
10.93.95.239 341187 RUNNING
unideploywebsite-0-0 unideploywebsite UniDeployWebsite manager cluster
10.93.95.239 341202 RUNNING
...
[All Processes: 16] [Running: 16] [Not Running: 0]
● If the value of Not Running is 0, all processes are running properly.
● If the value of Not Running is not 0, there are processes that are not running
or faulty.
NO TE
You can check status of a process by checking its value of Status.
● If Status is RUNNING, the process is running properly.
● If Status is STOPPED, the process is stopped.
● If Status is ABNORMAL, the process is abnormal. Run the ipmc_adm -cmd restartapp
-tenant manager command to restart the process. If the problem persists, contact
technical support.
Step 4 Check the running status of the hyperhaagent process.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the node where the hyperhaagent process instance
resides, as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to obtain the
IP address of a node, see 12.3 Querying the Management IP Address of the
Node Where a Process or Service Resides.
2. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: password for the ossadm user
3. Run the following commands to check the hyperhaagent process status:
> source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh
> ipmc_adm -cmd statusapp -app HyperHAAgent -tenant manager
Information similar to the following is displayed:
Process Name Process Type App Name Tenant Name Process Mode IP PID
Status
hyperhaagent-7-0 hyperhaagent HyperHAAgent manager cluster 10.93.95.239
156981 RUNNING
[All Processes: 1] [Running: 1] [Not Running: 0]
----End
13.8 Abnormal NTP Server Status
Symptom
On the PowerEcho, choose Maintenance > Time Management > Configure NTP
from the main menu. In the NTP server list, the time synchronization status of the

● The network is faulty.
● The time synchronization relationship between the NTP server and the
management node is abnormal.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser, ossadm, and root users of
the management node.
Troubleshooting Procedure
Step 1 Check whether the network connection between the management node and its
upstream NTP server is normal.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the management node as the sopuser user in SSH
mode.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, log in to Management0 and Management1, respectively, and perform the
following operations. For details about how to obtain the IP address of a node, see
12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a Node.
2. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: Password for the ossadm user
3. Check the component used by the NTP service.
– If the node is the management node, run the following command:
> cat /opt/oss/manager/var/etc/common/custom.cfg | grep
MGMT_NTP_TYPE
If information similar to the following is displayed, the NTP service uses
ntpd. If no command output is displayed, perform the operations in the
following scenario.
MGMT_NTP_TYPE=ntpd
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, the preceding command need to
be executed on both Management0 and Management1.
– If the node is a product node or one of the management nodes other
than Management0 and Management1, run the following command:
> bash /usr/local/osconfig/os/bin/getsupportntptype.sh
▪
If information similar to the following is displayed, the OS supports
ntpd and the NTP service uses ntpd:
["ntpd"]
▪
If information similar to the following is displayed, the OS supports
chrony and the NTP service uses chrony:
["chrony"]
▪
If information similar to the following is displayed, the OS supports

4. Check whether the network connection between the management node and
the upper-layer NTP server is normal, based on the component type obtained
in Step 1.3.
– If the ntpd component is used, run the following command:
> ntpq -np
remote refid st t when poll reach delay offset jitter
============================================================================
==
* x.x.x.x LOCAL(0) 6 u 90 128 377 0.199 -0.024 0.043
▪
If the values of reach, delay, offset, and jitter are not 0 in the
command output, the network connection between the management
node and its upstream NTP server is normal.
▪
If the values of reach, delay, offset, and jitter are 0 in the command
output, the network connection between the management node and
its upstream NTP server is abnormal.
– If the chrony component is used, run the following command:
> chronyc -n sources
M S Name/IP address Stratum Poll Reach LastRx Last sample
============================================================================
===
^ ? x.x.x.x 0 10 0 - +0ns[ +0ns] +/- 0ns
▪
In the command output, if the value of S is *, the network
connection between the management node and the upper-layer NTP
server is normal.
▪
If the value of S is ?, the network connection between the
management node and the upper-layer NTP server is abnormal.
Step 2 Check whether the NTP service of the upstream NTP server is normal.
Contact NTP engineers to check the NTP service status. Ensure that the NTP
service has started and has been provided for the PowerEcho.
Step 3 Check whether the NTP service of the management node is normal.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the management node as the sopuser user in SSH
mode.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, log in to Management0 and Management1 respectively and perform the
following operations. For details about how to obtain the IP address of a node, see
12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a Node.
2. Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: Password for the ossadm user
3. Check whether the NTP service is running, based on the component type
obtained in Step 1.3.
– If the ntpd component is used, run the following command:

If the command output contains active (running), the NTP service
on the node is started.
▪
Otherwise, the NTP service on the node is not started. Go to Step 3.4
to start the NTP service.
– If the chrony component is used, run the following command:
> systemctl status chronyd
▪
If the command output contains active (running), the NTP service
on the node is started.
▪
Otherwise, the NTP service on the node is not started. Go to Step 3.4
to start the NTP service.
4. Run the following command to switch to the root user:
> su - root
Password: Password for the root user
Run the following command to start the NTP service:
– If the ntpd component is used, run the following command:
# systemctl start ntpd
– If the chrony component is used, run the following command:
# systemctl start chronyd
5. Run the following command to exit the root user:
# exit
Step 4 If the preceding check result is normal but the time synchronization status of the
NTP server is still Abnormal, perform the following operations:
1. Log in to the PowerEcho. For details, see 1.2 Logging In to the PowerEcho.
2. On the PowerEcho, choose Maintenance > Time Management > Configure
NTP from the main menu. On the Configure NTP page, click Reconfigure.
3. In the Confirm dialog box, click Yes.
4. On the PowerEcho, choose Maintenance > Time Management > Configure
Time Zone and Time from the main menu. On the Configure Time Zone
and Time page, click Forcibly Synchronize.
NO TE
– If you deselect Automatically start the product databases and product services
after the modification in the Warning dialog box, manually start all product
services and product databases after the operations are complete. For details, see
3.1 Starting Product Services and Databases.
– After the time zone and time are forcibly synchronized, if the time difference
exceeds the value of Auto-logout if no activity within, client sessions are
automatically logged out.
5. Choose System > Task List from the main menu. Wait until the task for
forcibly synchronizing time zone and time is complete.
6. After the task is complete, if the time synchronization status of the NTP server
is still Abnormal, perform the following operations:
a. If the component obtained in Step 1.3 is chrony, go to Step 4.6.b. If the

Configure NTP from the main menu.
c. If Encryption Mode of an NTP server is NTPv4 authentication, on the
Configure NTP page, change the value of Key of the NTP server as
prompted.
d. Perform Step 4.4 to Step 4.5 to forcibly synchronize the time zone and
time.
----End
13.9 Managing Passwords in the Weak Password
Dictionary
For security purposes, do not use passwords that are vulnerable to cracking.
Passwords in the weak password dictionary are not allowed, and you can add
passwords to or change existing passwords in the weak password dictionary as
required.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and root users of the
management node.
Procedure
Step 1 Export the weak password dictionary to a user-defined file.
1. Use PuTTY to log in to the node with OS user password to be changed, as the
sopuser user in SSH mode. For details about how to obtain the IP address of
a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a Node
2. Run the following command to switch to the root user:
> su - root
Password: password for the root user
3. Run the following command to export the default dictionary password to
the /usr/share/cracklib/dictionary.txt file:
# cracklib-unpacker /usr/share/cracklib/pw_dict > /usr/share/cracklib/
dictionary.txt
NO TE
The path and file name /usr/share/cracklib/dictionary.txt can be customized. Change
it as require, but ensure that the format is .txt.
Step 2 Check or change passwords in the weak password dictionary as required.
● Checking passwords in the weak password dictionary
# cat /usr/share/cracklib/dictionary.txt
● Changing passwords in the weak password dictionary
a. Run the following command to open the /usr/share/cracklib/
dictionary.txt file using the vi editor:

press Esc to exit the editing mode, and enter :wq! to save the
modification and exit the vi editor.
c. Run the following command to update the dictionary:
# create-cracklib-dict /usr/share/cracklib/dictionary.txt
Information similar to the following is displayed. If the two numbers are
the same, the update is successful. Otherwise, contact technical support
engineers.
1916950 1916950
d. Run the following command to delete the /usr/share/cracklib/
dictionary.txt file:
# cd /usr/share/cracklib/
# rm -rf dictionary.txt
e. Run the following command to exit the root user:
# exit
----End
13.10 Querying a Product Name
You can query the product name based on the IP address of a node.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the passwords for the sopuser and ossadm users of the node
to be queried.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the node to be queried as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Run the following commands to query the name of the product of the node:
> source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh
> ipmc_adm -cmd statusapp
Information similar to the following is displayed. Tenant Name indicates the
product name of the node.
Process Name ... App Name Tenant Name Process Mode IP PID Status
switchmgrapp-0-0 ... DBHASwitchService cdo cluster 10.243.4.18 9691 RUNNING
mczkapp-0-0 ... MCZKService cdo multi 10.243.4.18 18956 RUNNING
...

13.11 Product Node Faults
Symptom
On the PowerEcho, choose Maintenance > Operation and Maintenance
Management > Panoramic Monitoring. In the navigation pane, choose Node
Monitoring. In the upper left corner of the Node Monitoring page, select the
product and check whether the connection status of the node under the product is
Disconnected.
Possible Causes
● The network is faulty.
● The node is powered off.
Troubleshooting Procedure
Step 1 Execute the check items and check methods in Table 13-2 and rectify the fault
according to the corresponding troubleshooting methods.
NO TE
The product node faults are caused by complicated causes. This section provides basic
troubleshooting methods for rectifying the fault. If the faults persist after you perform the
following operations, collect the fault information and contact technical support.

13.12 Viewing Certificate Details
This section uses the CA certificate as an example to describe how to view the
details (such as the validity period) about a certificate. To view details about other
certificates, replace the path in the command as required.
Prerequisites
You have obtained the IP address of the management node and the passwords for
the sopuser and ossadm users.

Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the management node as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in cluster mode, that is, there are multiple management
nodes, perform operations on Management0 or Management1. For details about how to
obtain the IP address of a node, see 12.5 Querying the Management IP Address of a
Node.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Run the following commands to check the certificate details. In the
command, /opt/oss/manager/var/ca is the directory for storing the CA certificate.
Replace it as required.
> cd /opt/oss/manager/apps/UniEPService/tools/common
> bash view_cert_info.sh -path /opt/oss/manager/var/ca/ca.cer
NO TE
If the PowerEcho is deployed in single-server mode, and the management node and
product node are the same node, replace UniEPService with UniEPLiteService.
Information similar to the following is displayed. For details about the parameters
in the command output, see Table 13-3.
Certificate:
Data:
Version: 3 (0x2)
Serial Number:
76:3c:04:ed:92:86:c1:6c:4f:69:31:09:73:25:83:60:30
Signature Algorithm: xxxxx
Issuer: C=CN, ST=GuangDong, O=Huawei Technologies, OU=xx Department, CN=huaweiossCA
Validity
Not Before: Mar 10 09:39:00 2017 GMT
Not After : Mar 10 08:39:00 2042 GMT

Parameter Description
Version Format version of the X.509 certificate.
Serial Number Unique numeric identifier that the CA assigns to the
certificate.
Issuer Certificate issuing authority.
Validity Validity period of a certificate. The value includes:
● Date and time when the certificate takes effect.
● Date and time when the certificate expires.
Each time you use a certificate, check whether the
certificate is within the validity period. If the certificate
has expired or is about to expire, update the certificate
immediately.
Subject Certificate user.
Subject Public Key Example: rsaEncryption.
Info
extension Additional information about the certificate.
Signature Algorithm Signature algorithm used when the CA issues a
certificate.

13.13 Converting the Certificate Format
This section describes how to convert a certificate file from a common format to
the required format to meet the requirements for certificate updates.
Prerequisites
You have obtained a certificate from the CA.
.pem to .cer
If the obtained certificate is in .pem format, rename it to the corresponding file
name. For example, if the trust certificate is xxx.pem, rename it to trust.cer.
.crt to .cer
Step 1 Double-click the .crt certificate file.
Step 2 Click the Details tab and click Copy to File in the lower right corner.

Next.
Step 5 Click Browse. In the Save As dialog box, select the certificate storage path on your
local PC, enter the certificate name, and click Save.
Step 6 Click Next.
Step 7 In the dialog box that is displayed, click Finish. If "The export was successful." is
displayed, the conversion is successful.
----End
.der to .cer
Step 1 Use FileZilla to upload the certificate file in .der format (for example, input.der)
to the /home/sopuser directory on any of the management nodes, as the sopuser
user in SFTP mode. For details, see 13.2 Transferring Files Using FileZilla.
Step 2 Use PuTTY to log in to the node described in Step 1, as the sopuser user in SSH
mode.
Step 3 Run the following command to set the permission for the input.der file:
> chmod 600 /home/sopuser/input.der
Step 4 Run the following command to convert the .der certificate file to a .cer file:
> openssl x509 -in input.der -inform DER -out server.cer
Step 5 Use FileZilla to download the server.cer file in the /home/sopuser directory to
your local PC, as the sopuser user in SFTP mode.
Step 6 Use PuTTY to run the following commands to delete temporary files:
> cd /home/sopuser
> rm -f input.der
> rm -f server.cer
----End
13.14 Merging Trust Certificate Files
If the trust certificate to be uploaded for update on the PowerEcho contains the
root certificate and the intermediate certificate, you need to merge the trust
certificate files by following the instructions in this section.
Prerequisites
You have obtained a new identity certificate.
Procedure

2. Click the root certificate at the top layer, and then click View Certificates.
3. Click the Details tab and click Copy to File in the lower right corner.
4. In the dialog box that is displayed, click Next.
5. In the dialog box that is displayed, select Base-64 encoded X.509(.CER), and
click Next.
6. Click Browse. In the Save As dialog box, select the file path, enter the
certificate name rootCA.cer, and click Save.
7. Click Next.
8. In the dialog box that is displayed, click Finish. If "The export was successful."
is displayed, the certificate is exported successfully.
Step 2 Export the intermediate certificate.
1. Double-click the identity certificate and click the Certificate Path tab.
2. Click the certificate under the root certificate, and then click View
Certificates.
3. Click the Details tab and click Copy to File in the lower right corner.
4. In the dialog box that is displayed, click Next.
5. In the dialog box that is displayed, select Base-64 encoded X.509(.CER), and
click Next.
6. Click Browse. In the Save As dialog box, select the file path, enter the
certificate name subCA.cer, and click Save.
7. Click Next.
8. In the dialog box that is displayed, click Finish. If "The export was successful."
is displayed, the certificate is exported successfully.
NO TE
If there are multiple intermediate certificates, export them by referring to Step 2.
Step 3 Generate the trust certificate file trust.cer.
1. Create a file named trust.cer and open it with a text editor.
2. Open the trust certificate subCA.cer with a text editor and copy its file
content to trust.cer.
3. Use a text editor to open the trust certificate rootCA.cer and copy the file
content to trust.cer.
4. Save trust.cer, which is the trust certificate file merging the root certificate
and the intermediate certificate.
NO TE
If there are multiple intermediate certificates, copy the file content to trust.cer by referring
to Step 3.2.
----End

13.15 Manually Authenticating SFTP Fingerprint of a
Backup Server
When configuring backup server parameters for the first time, to improve data
security of the PowerEcho, SFTP fingerprint authentication must be performed
between the backup server and all nodes. The PowerEcho can automatically
authenticate the SFTP fingerprint. You can also manually authenticate the SFTP
fingerprint by referring to this section.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the password for the sopuser user of the nodes where
SFTP fingerprint authentication has not been performed.
● You have obtained the IP address of the backup server.
● You have obtained the username of the backup server.
● You have obtained the IP address of the node where SFTP fingerprint
authentication has not been performed.
NO TE
On the PowerEcho, choose Backup and Restore > Configuration > Configure Backup
Parameters from the main menu. When adding a backup server, click and then
click Details to obtain the IP address of the node where SFTP fingerprint
authentication has not been performed.
Procedure
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the node where SFTP fingerprint authentication has not
been performed as the sopuser user in SSH mode. For details, see 13.1 Logging
In to a Server Using PuTTY.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the ossadm user:
> su - ossadm
Password: password for the ossadm user
Step 3 Run the following command to perform SFTP fingerprint authentication:
> sftp backup server username@[backup server IP address]
If the following information is displayed, enter yes and press Enter:
...
Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no)?
Step 4 If the following information is displayed, enter the password for the backup server
user and press Enter.
Backup server username@backup server IP address's password:***********
Step 5 If the following information is displayed, SFTP fingerprint authentication is
successful.
Connected to backup server IP address

13.16 Verifying File Integrity Using a P7S Signature File
If a software package has a P7S signature file, you can manually verify the
integrity of the file on NetEco after uploading the package to NetEco.
Procedure
NO TE
The following operations use/home/sopuser/product-domain_version_Aaaa.zip and its P7S
signature file as an example.
Step 1 Use PuTTY to log in to the node to be verified as the sopuser user in SSH mode.
For details, see 13.1 Logging In to a Server Using PuTTY.
Step 2 Run the following command to switch to the root user. If another user performs
operations on product-domain_version_Aaaa.zip, switch to the user.
> su - root
Password: Password for the root user
Step 3 Run the following commands to create a directory for product-
domain_version_Aaaa.zip:
# cd /opt
# rm -rf Aaaa
# mkdir /opt/Aaaa
Step 4 Move product-domain_version_Aaaa.zip and its corresponding P7S signature file
to the /opt/Aaaa directory.
# mv /home/sopuser/product-domain_version_Aaaa.zip* /opt/Aaaa/
Step 5 Run the following commands to set the owner group and modify the file
permission. The file permission must be consistent with the actual service
operation permission.
# chown -h root:ossgroup /opt/Aaaa/ -RL
# chmod 700 /opt/Aaaa/
# chmod 500 /opt/Aaaa/*
Step 6 Run the following commands to verify the signature file:
# cd /opt/Aaaa/
# /usr/bin/hwcmstool --verify --source product-domain_version_Aaaa.zip --sign
product-domain_version_Aaaa.zip.p7s --type RELEASE_SIGN
If information similar to the following is displayed, the verification is successful.
Then perform subsequent service operations.
info: set local crl to default: /etc/ssl/certs/HuaweiRootCAsipCRLs_Release.crl, Sign type: RELEASE_SIGN
SignCLI Info: Successfully verified.
crl verify success
Otherwise, download and upload product-domain_version_Aaaa.zip and its

technical support. You can perform subsequent service operations only after the
signature file verification is successful. Otherwise, security risks may occur.
Step 7 Run the following command to exit the root user:
# exit
----End

The list above provides all application database users of services deployed on the
PowerEcho. If a new service is deployed on the PowerEcho, the corresponding application
database user will be created. The list needs to be updated.
14.2 File System of the NetEco
This section describes the file system of the NetEco. The file system mainly
includes the information about the directory structure of the NetEco software.

Directory Description
/opt/oss Root directory for installing the NetEco
program, including subdirectories of the
PowerEcho, the NetEco, and logs
/opt/zenith Zenith installation directory (service
database)
/opt/neteco Directory for storing the NetEco service
data
/opt/neteco_backup NetEco backup directory
/opt/NetEcoTools Directory for storing the NetEco
uninstallation script
/opt/pub Directory for storing large files generated
during the NetEco installation
/opt/patch_manager Third-party patch framework running
directory for the NetEco
/opt/redis Redis installation directory
/opt/repo Directory for storing the NetEco product
package and deployment tool
/opt/share Shared data directory between services
